Foliage measurements remain near the noise floor at -76 to -82 dBFS with limited spectral structure, consistent with strong canopy attenuation. Urban measurements show multipath activity across a 30 dB dynamic range, overlapping WiFi channels, and frequent ISM-band interference. Indoor measurements show dominant WiFi channels, an estimated 20 to 25 dB building entry loss relative to outdoor conditions, and an 8 to 10 dB higher interference floor caused by structural reflections. The system sustained 75 to 85 MB/s write throughput with no dropped samples or buffer underruns, while GNSS synchronization remained below one second with meter-level positioning. These results show that a portable, cost-effective SDR platform can produce high-fidelity, geotagged IQ datasets for spectrum characterization, interference analysis, radio environment mapping, and environment-aware wireless research.

</details>


### [88] [Physically-Aware Preemptive Virtual Channels for Deadlock-Free AXI Networks-on-Chip](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01430)
*Lorenzo Leone,Luca Colagrande,Luca Benini*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper addresses deadlock-free traffic separation for AXI4 systems in Networks-on-Chip (NoCs), proposing Preemptive Virtual Channels that reduce physical link resources significantly while maintaining comparable performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scaling of many-core System-on-Chips demands high memory bandwidth in NoCs, while ensuring deadlock freedom despite protocol-level dependencies in AXI4 systems.

Method: Four deadlock-free traffic-class separation schemes are evaluated, including a multiplane baseline and three VC-based designs. A new Preemptive VC-based architecture is proposed.

Result: Preemptive Virtual Channels save up to 76% of link resources compared to the multiplane design, with only 3% router area overhead and comparable operating frequency.

Conclusion: The work achieves efficient deadlock-free traffic separation for AXI4 systems, highlighting a resource-saving design with minimal area trade-offs.

Abstract: As many-core Systems-on-Chip (SoCs) continue to scale, Networks-on-Chip (NoCs) must sustain increasingly high memory bandwidth while preserving deadlock freedom. In AXI4 systems, protocol-level dependencies between read and write traffic can create circular waits at the network endpoints, even when the routing algorithm itself is deadlock-free. Decoupling these traffic classes avoids such dependencies, but exposes a key implementation trade-off: multiplane NoCs duplicate wide physical links and increase routing pressure, whereas conventional Virtual Channel (VC) routers add substantial control complexity, area, and timing overhead. This work revisits this trade-off for modern wide-link NoCs. We evaluate four deadlock-free AXI4 traffic-class separation schemes: a multiplane baseline and three lightweight VC-based designs. Among these designs, we propose Preemptive VCs, a physically-aware architecture that can save up to 76% of link resources with comparable frequency and only 3% router area overhead relative to the multiplane design.

</details>


### [89] [MxGLUT: A Reconfigurable LUT-Centric Broadcast Dataflow Accelerator for Mixed-Precision GEMM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01607)
*Weiyu Zhou,Chen Ding,Mingyuan Liu,Liangyu Gan,Yukun Feng,Hao Jia,Haoming Chu,Lirong Zheng,Ning Ma,Yuxiang Huan*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper introduces MxGLUT, a LUT-based accelerator designed for efficient LLM inference, achieving significant area, energy, and computational efficiencies while supporting both FP8-INT4 and FP8-FP8 modes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome inefficiency in LLM inference caused by weight-only quantization and non-unified mixed-precision execution in existing accelerators.

Method: MxGLUT employs a reconfigurable LUT-centric broadcast (RLB) dataflow and mixed-precision LUT-based processing elements (MxLPEs) to unify FP8-INT4 and FP8-FP8 computations, avoiding separate FP datapaths or FP multipliers.

Result: Synthesized in 28nm CMOS, MxGLUT achieves significant reductions in multiplier area (up to 56.92%) and power consumption (up to 78.35%), with impressive area and energy efficiency. It also delivers improved latency and energy reductions in LLM inference phases, maintaining low perplexity increases.

Conclusion: MxGLUT offers an efficient and unified solution for LLM inference, demonstrating impressive computational and power efficiencies while supporting multiple data precision formats.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) inference suffers from growing inefficiency across the prefill and decode phases, especially under weight-only quantization, where activations remain in FP8 while weights are compressed to low-bit integers. Existing LUT-based accelerators mainly target FP8-INT4 computation and still rely on separate floating-point (FP) datapaths for attention GEMM operations, leading to redundant hardware and non-unified mixed-precision execution. Moreover, their static dataflows are poorly matched to the distinct prefill and decode phases. To address these challenges, we propose MxGLUT, a reconfigurable LUT-centric broadcast (RLB) dataflow accelerator built on mixed-precision LUT-based processing elements (MxLPEs). Guided by a unified LUT-based execution framework, MxGLUT organizes both FP8-INT4 and FP8-FP8 GEMMs under a single LUT-based compute mechanism without dedicated FP multipliers or additional FP datapaths, and further adopts the RLB dataflow that localizes heavy partial-sum accumulation during the prefill phase and exploits weight reuse in the decode phase. Synthesized in UMC $28\,\mathrm{nm}$ CMOS at $200~\mathrm{MHz}$, MxGLUT reduces multiplier area by up to $56.92\%$ and power by up to $77.07\%$ and $78.35\%$ in FP8-INT4 and FP8-FP8 modes, respectively. At the accelerator level, MxGLUT achieves an area efficiency of $0.492~\mathrm{TFLOPS/mm^2}$ and an energy efficiency of $11.58~\mathrm{TFLOPS/W}$, while adding native FP8-FP8 support incurs only $2.57\%$ and $3.34\%$ reductions in area and energy efficiency, respectively, relative to the FP8-INT4-only FIGLUT baseline. Across the Llama family, MxGLUT achieves up to $2.16\times$ and $1.49\times$ latency speedup, and reduces normalized energy to $0.44\times$ and $0.71\times$ in prefill and decode, respectively, with at most $1.70\%$ perplexity increase.

</details>


### [90] [3DLS: A 3D Logic-Stacked Architecture for Disaggregated LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01617)
*Jaehun Lee,In-Jun Jung,Joo-Young Kim*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper proposes 3DLS, a 3D-stacked chiplet architecture, to improve the performance of LLM serving by solving traffic contention issues from mixed-traffic on traditional architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the performance bottlenecks in LLM serving caused by traffic contention on the die-to-die interconnect used for both KV-cache transfers and tensor parallel (TP) collectives in conventional planar chiplet architectures.

Method: Designing 3DLS, a logic-on-logic 3D-stacked chiplet architecture, which reroutes KV-cache transfers through vertical interconnects while keeping TP collectives on lateral interconnects, thus physically separating traffic classes.

Result: 3DLS delivers up to 1.49x higher throughput and 60.2% lower end-to-end latency compared to a planar baseline, and also outperforms a workload-aware planar baseline with up to 1.17x throughput and 31.4% lower latency.

Conclusion: Physical isolation of traffic classes is crucial for optimizing chiplet-based LLM serving systems, and 3DLS demonstrates its effectiveness in overcoming mixed-traffic contention to improve performance.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) serving increasingly combines prefill-decode (PD) disaggregation with tensor parallelism (TP) to support large models and long contexts. In conventional 2D/2.5D chiplet architectures, layer-wise prefill-to-decode KV-cache transfer decode-side TP collectives share the same lateral die-to-die (D2D) interconnect, creating mixed-traffic contention on the decode critical path. This contention increases communication latency, prolongs token generation intervals, and degrades end-to-end serving performance. We propose 3DLS, a logic-on-logic 3D-stacked chiplet architecture that separates traffic classes by routing KV-cache transfers through vertical interconnects while preserving decode-side TP collectives on the lateral D2D fabric. 3DLS achieves up to 1.49$\times$ throughput and 60.2\% lower end-to-end (E2E) latency over the shared-fabric planar baseline, and still achieves up to 1.17$\times$ throughput and 31.4\% lower E2E latency over a workload-aware priority-managed planar baseline. These results highlight that physical isolation is an important design principle for future chiplet-based PD-disaggregated LLM serving systems.

</details>


### [91] [Approximate Attention Weighting for Sustainable FPGA-Based Vision Transformer Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01798)
*Muhammad Usman,Shujaat Khan,Dorit Merhof*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a resource-efficient, BRAM-free unit for FPGA implementation of Vision Transformers (ViTs), achieving accurate attention approximation without hardware overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying Vision Transformers on small FPGAs is hard due to the hardware costs of the softmax function in self-attention.

Method: The study proposes a BRAM-free design that approximates the natural exponential in softmax using a piecewise-linear function implemented with distributed LUTs.

Result: The core uses 1444 LUTs and 77 DSPs without BRAM, achieving accuracy within 0.20% of exact softmax on ViT models.

Conclusion: The solution enables energy-efficient ViT inference on edge-AI platforms with constrained hardware resources.

Abstract: Vision Transformers have reshaped computer vision by using self-attention to capture global context across image regions. This makes them attractive for edge visual inspection and monitoring in applications such as renewable-energy infrastructure, industrial quality control, medical imaging, and autonomous-system sensing. However, deploying ViTs on small FPGAs remains challenging because the softmax stage in self-attention requires exponential evaluation and normalization, which are costly in hardware. Existing implementations often rely on CORDIC pipelines or BRAM-based look-up tables, increasing area and power consumption. This paper presents a BRAM-free approximate attention-weighting unit for FPGA-based ViT inference. The proposed design approximates the natural exponential in softmax using a 16-segment piecewise-linear function implemented entirely with distributed LUT fabric. Unlike base-2 approximations, the natural-exponential formulation preserves the pre-trained attention temperature and avoids model-specific recalibration. Implemented on a Xilinx Zynq-7020, the complete attention-row core uses 1444 LUTs, 77 DSPs, and no BRAM, while hardware-accurate emulation shows accuracy within a \(0.20\%\) absolute top-1 difference from the exact-softmax reference on ViT-family models. These results demonstrate the potential of the proposed core for energy-efficient ViT inference on resource-constrained edge-AI platforms.

</details>


### [92] [Probabilistic Memory for Trustworthy Edge Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02465)
*Likai Pei,Jiahao Zheng,Xueji Zhao,Emilie Ye,Jianbo Liu,Hanqing Tao,Ming-Yen Lee,Ruiyang Qin,Yiyu Shi,Shimeng Yu,X. Sharon Hu,Ningyuan Cao*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper proposes probabilistic memory (p-MEM), a novel hardware approach to improve Gaussian random number generation (GRNG) throughput and reduce energy and latency for probabilistic AI applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the bottlenecks in probabilistic computation, specifically limited GRNG throughput and associated inefficiencies, which hinder the development of trustworthy edge intelligence.

Method: The paper introduces p-MEM, a memory primitive that stores distribution parameters and samples directly, while using a simulator to explore device options, memory specs, and technology configurations.

Result: The study demonstrates that p-MEM achieves over 1000 GSa/s/mm^2 GRNG throughput and integrates into CPU/GPU systems, reducing instructions, sampling latency, and energy consumption significantly in Bayesian neural network tasks.

Conclusion: p-MEM offers a scalable hardware solution to enhance probabilistic AI computation, making trustworthy edge intelligence more effective and efficient.

Abstract: Probabilistic computation plays an important role in trustworthy edge intelligence to quantify uncertainty, enhance robustness, reconstruct data, and protect privacy, but its adoption is limited by the orders-of-magnitude data throughput gap between Gaussian random number generation (GRNG) and computation, as well as instruction overhead. This paper introduces probabilistic memory (p-MEM), a unified memory primitive that stores distribution parameters, such as mean and standard deviation, and samples directly at the native memory bandwidth, where deterministic data becomes the zero-variance special case. Using a layout-validated p-MEM simulator, we comprehensively explore device choices, memory specifications, and technology nodes, showing that p-MEM can achieve more than 1000 GSa/s/mm^2 GRNG throughput, including memory-array access. Integrated into CPU/GPU systems, p-MEM reduces instruction count by up to 2.19x/4.37x, sampling latency by 562x/3.45x, and energy by 295.5x/3.53x for Bayesian neural network workloads, providing a scalable hardware substrate for trustworthy probabilistic AI.

</details>


<div id='cs.CL'></div>

# cs.CL [[Back]](#toc)

### [93] [TokenScope: Token-Level Explainability and Interpretability for Code-Oriented Tasks in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01235)
*Amirreza Esmaeili,Fatemeh Fard*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces TokenScope, a tool that provides real-time insights and interactive exploration for understanding token-level decisions of LLMs during code generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of decoding-time signals, fine-grained uncertainty measures, and mechanisms for exploring alternative generation paths in existing tools for analyzing LLMs, especially during code generation.

Method: The authors design TokenScope, a tool that integrates token-level metrics, attention patterns, structural information, and code-aware analysis through mechanisms like interactive token replacement and counterfactual branching using abstract syntax trees.

Result: TokenScope offers unified decoding-time signals and code-specific structural insights, facilitating a more systematic analysis of LLM decision-making during code generation.

Conclusion: The development of TokenScope bridges the gap in LLM interpretability, providing a practical and interactive framework for researchers and practitioners to investigate and analyze LLM behavior during code generation tasks.

Abstract: Understanding how Large Language Models (LLMs) make token-level decisions during code generation remains a major challenge for both researchers and practitioners. While recent tools provide insights into model internals or generation outcomes, they often lack decoding-time signals, fine-grained uncertainty measures, and interactive mechanisms for exploring alternative generation paths. We present TokenScope, an interactive interpretability and analysis tool for decoder-based LLMs that exposes token-level metrics, attention patterns, and structural information during generation. TokenScope supports interactive token replacement, counterfactual branching, and code-aware aggregation via abstract syntax trees. By unifying decoding-time signals with structural program analysis, TokenScope enables systematic investigation of LLM behaviour during code generation.

</details>


### [94] [Safeguarding LLM Agents from Misalignment through Provenance Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01236)
*Yining She,Yiliang Liang,Eunsuk Kang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ProvenanceGuard, a framework to ensure LLM agents' actions align with user intentions by analyzing misalignment systematically using provenance-based reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Misalignment in LLM tool invocations can lead to harmful outcomes, and existing frameworks relying on LLMs for judgment are inconsistent and hard to audit.

Method: ProvenanceGuard uses a provenance-based conceptual framework to detect and analyze misalignment before executing tool calls, incorporating a multi-stage pipeline for systematic evaluation.

Result: ProvenanceGuard significantly reduces misalignment error rates compared to traditional baselines on two benchmarks (Agent-SafetyBench and WorkBench) across multiple LLMs.

Conclusion: Structured provenance-based reasoning offers an effective approach to safeguarding LLM actions by improving alignment and reducing unnecessary interventions while maintaining task success.

Abstract: As LLM agents gain increasing access to powerful tools, ensuring that their actions are aligned with the user's intent becomes critical. When an agent's proposed tool invocation deviates from the user's intent -- a phenomenon called misalignment -- it may lead to harmful consequences that are difficult to undo. Existing runtime guardrails rely on an LLM-as-a-judge paradigm that lacks a systematic framework for reasoning about alignment, often producing judgments that are inconsistent or difficult to audit. Motivated by provenance analysis, we propose a provenance-based conceptual framework that formalizes misalignment detection as determining whether a proposed tool call is supported by traceable evidence in the agent's context. Building on this framework, we propose ProvenanceGuard, a multi-stage pipeline that analyzes the agent's action for three types of misalignment before the selected tool is executed and only allows the action to take place when it is considered aligned with the user's input query. We evaluated our proposed approach on two different benchmarks, Agent-SafetyBench and WorkBench, across 10 backbone LLMs. Compared to the LLM-as-a-judge baseline, ProvenanceGuard reduces error rate on misaligned traces from 42.9% to 1.8% on Agent-SafetyBench and from 32.1% to 17.3% on WorkBench, while reducing intervention burden on task-successful traces from 30.5% to 12.8% and introducing no statistically significant increase in unnecessary interventions on aligned traces. These results demonstrate that structured, provenance-based reasoning provides an effective and practical foundation for safeguarding LLM agents from misalignment.

</details>


### [95] [Kara: Efficient Reasoning LLM Serving via Sliding-Window KV Cache Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01237)
*Shen Han,Yuyang Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses memory and throughput challenges in reasoning language models caused by long chain-of-thought decoding by proposing a new KV cache compression method named Kara.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning language models generate extensive KV caches during decoding, leading to high latency and throughput constraints. Existing KV cache compression methods have limitations in effectively balancing information retention and throughput improvement.

Method: The authors introduce Kara, a sliding-window KV cache compression method that focuses on recently generated content and uses bidirectional attention for selective KV pair retention. It also includes a Token2Chunk module for flexible-sized chunk preservation. This method is integrated with PagedAttention to create KvLLM, a framework aimed at reducing memory usage and improving throughput.

Result: Experiments confirm that Kara and KvLLM provide consistent improvements in memory efficiency and output throughput, addressing the issues present in existing methods.

Conclusion: Kara and KvLLM offer a robust solution for optimizing KV cache utilization and decoding efficiency in reasoning language models, overcoming the limitations of prior approaches.

Abstract: Reasoning language models often generate long chain-of-thought (CoT), which accumulates a massive KV cache during the decoding phase and incurs high decoding latency and limited throughput. To address these issues, KV cache compression has emerged as a promising technique for reducing memory overhead by selectively removing unimportant KV pairs while preserving useful ones for subsequent decoding. Nevertheless, we identify two key limitations in existing KV cache compression methods: 1) their threshold-triggered compression policy may provide limited throughput improvement or even reduce throughput, and may fully eliminate KV pairs from certain blocks of the sequence, potentially worsening information loss. 2) they typically retain either isolated KV pairs or fixed-size chunks with rigid boundaries, failing to preserve important flexible-sized chunks at arbitrary token positions. To overcome these limitations, we propose Kara, a sliding-window KV cache compression method that performs decoding-time compression by operating only on the recently generated context. Kara leverages bidirectional attention to score and select informative KV pairs in the window. To enable flexible preservation of important semantic information, we design a Token2Chunk module to expand a subset of selected KV pairs into chunks. Furthermore, we adapt Kara to PagedAttention and develop KvLLM, an inference framework built upon vLLM, which reduces KV cache memory usage and effectively improves output throughput. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent performance improvements of proposed Kara and KvLLM.

</details>


### [96] [SPARCLE: SPeaker-aware Aligned Representations via Contrastive Language Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01238)
*Priyam Mazumdar,Yurii Halychanskyi,Steven Guo,Mark Hasegawa-Johnson,Volodymyr Kindratenko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SPARCLE, a model that uses speaker-aware grapheme representations to enhance text-to-speech (TTS) systems, showing superior performance especially in low-resource scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current low-resource text-to-speech (TTS) systems struggle due to limitations of phoneme-based models and grapheme-based alternatives, particularly in capturing speaker-specific acoustic variations.

Method: SPARCLE aligns graphemes with Wav2Vec2 acoustic representations using a contrastive training objective, while conditioning on speaker identity, effectively replacing traditional grapheme-to-phoneme systems in TTS applications.

Result: The proposed SPARCLE model significantly reduces word error rates and demonstrates improved TTS generation quality in low-resource settings compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: By leveraging precise, speaker-aware acoustic realizations, SPARCLE overcomes limitations of prior models and showcases its potential as a more effective replacement for traditional G2P systems.

Abstract: Recent advances in speech synthesis have shifted from phoneme representations to direct grapheme modeling. While phonemes address the one-to-many mapping between text and acoustics, they rely on grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) systems that fail to capture speaker-specific acoustic variation. Prior work demonstrates that grapheme-based models outperform phoneme-based systems at scale, but not in low-resource settings.
  In this paper, we propose SPARCLE, a speaker-aware grapheme representation model that enriches characters with their precise acoustic realizations. SPARCLE is trained with a contrastive objective to align graphemes with corresponding Wav2Vec2 acoustic representations while conditioned on speaker identity. The resulting model serves as a replacement to G2P systems for downstream text-to-speech (TTS) tasks. We demonstrate that SPARCLE improves generation quality, reducing word error rates by half in extreme low-resource settings compared to standard grapheme-based models.

</details>


### [97] [Breaking Safety at the Token Boundary: How BPE Tokenization Creates Exploitable Gaps in LLM Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01239)
*Tung-Ling Li,Hongliang Liu,Yuhao Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper examines vulnerabilities in LLMs caused by character-level perturbations that bypass safety alignment through BPE tokenization, and demonstrates how fragmented inputs can compromise safety-critical responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore why and how modern LLMs remain vulnerable to character-level perturbations that fragment safety-critical words and cause them to generate harmful outputs, despite human-readable prompts and safety-alignment measures.

Method: The authors investigate the issue through a multi-faceted approach: testing LLMs across different families, analyzing dataset representation of fragmented prompts, conducting patching techniques to localize disrupted signals, creating targeted mutations to analyze safety word roles, and performing novel diagnostics to differentiate failures from global collapse.

Result: Character-level perturbations targeting token fragmentation successfully bypass safety mechanisms in LLMs, flipping refusal triggers and generating harmful outputs in 48% of cases (range 29-65% across models). Specific safety-token fragmentation emerges as a key vulnerability and is underrepresented in current datasets.

Conclusion: While adding fragmented prompts during training can reduce attack success rates (ASR) for some LLM families, it fails to fully address the vulnerability without inducing global refusal increases, indicating that current alignment methods are insufficient without more targeted solutions for selective safety enforcement.

Abstract: Character-level perturbations bypass safety alignment in modern LLMs despite leaving prompts human-readable. We identify and test a central structural mechanism: BPE tokenization fragments safety-critical words into sub-word pieces, and the three public alignment datasets we surveyed contain no intentionally fragmented inputs. The mechanism is a chain, tested end-to-end on five model families (Qwen-3-4B, Qwen-2.5-7B, Gemma-3-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B). An optimization targeting safety-token fragmentation flips the first-token refusal trigger on 80-100% of refused HarmBench prompts, with 48% of those flips producing genuinely harmful outputs (per-model 29-65%; gap-vs-behavior ROC-AUC 0.66-0.98, pooled 0.84). Activation patching localizes the disrupted signal to the last ${\sim}30\%$ of layers; an alignment-data scan finds zero fragmented prompts among 30,000 examples (positive-control recall $\geq 99\%$ at attack-relevant intensities); and targeted-mutation experiments isolate safety words as the disruption locus. On the defense side, a 68-cell grid (55 trained checkpoints) shows that no DPO configuration achieves seed- and pool-stable ASR closure on the three families with closed pool-size confounds. SFT trained on fragmented prompts closes ASR on 3/5 families but only via global collapse that raises refusal on benign prompts as well, indicating the missing distribution is necessary but not sufficient under the LoRA-16 recipe we tested. To distinguish selective repair from global collapse, we introduce Conv-Benign, a candidate paired diagnostic. All ASR claims are 3-judge-calibrated (cell rankings stable across judges; absolute levels $\pm$18pp; see App.~B.13).

</details>


### [98] [Prompt Framing Distorts Count-Based Evaluation of LLM Error Detection: Evidence from Numeric Anchoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01240)
*Dekun Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper highlights the issue of F1 Inflation in LLM error detection, showing that F1 scores can rise without improved span localization. It introduces ErrorBench for stress-testing prompt-induced count distortions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate and diagnose F1 Inflation in language model evaluations, shedding light on the gap between F1 score improvements and true error localization.

Method: The authors created ErrorBench, a stress-test protocol analyzing six LLMs using five prompt conditions across thousands of data points. Different scoring systems like CoNLL-2014 and ERRANT 3.0.0 were employed.

Result: The study found noticeable F1 Inflation under controlled conditions. For instance, a prompt shift increased Count-F1 significantly more (+0.21) than ERRANT F0.5 (+0.04), indicating misleading metrics in certain setups.

Conclusion: To ensure accurate LLM evaluations, metrics should avoid bias from pre-filled error counts and incorporate span-aware methods alongside traditional count-based measures.

Abstract: Count-based F1 is widely used as a proxy for LLM error-detection quality, but this paper shows that it can rise dramatically without a corresponding improvement in span localization, a gap termed F1 Inflation. The paper introduces ErrorBench, a controlled stress-test protocol for prompt-induced count distortion. ErrorBench evaluates six contemporary LLMs under five prompt conditions over 4,290 responses from 143 CoNLL-2014 passages. Under CoNLL-2014 M2-style scoring, anchored prompts produce up to 0.79 points of F1 Inflation, and up to 0.96 under strict matching. A 100-passage replication using the official ERRANT 3.0.0 pipeline and multi-reference scoring reproduces the pattern: averaged over six models, the Blind-to-Anchored prompt shift raises Count-F1 by +0.21 while raising multi-reference ERRANT F0.5 by only +0.04. The study finds larger count responses in highly instruction-compliant GPT/Claude systems and smaller responses in the Gemini family under this stress-test protocol. The findings suggest that LLM proofreading and document-review evaluations should avoid pre-populated error counts and should report span-aware metrics alongside count-based metrics.

</details>


### [99] [Mapping Text to Multiplex Graph: Prompt Compression as Lévy Walk-Guided Graph Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01241)
*Yaxin Gao,Yao Lu,Jinhong Deng,Jiaqi Nie,Zhe Tang,Jian Zhang,Zhaowei Zhu,Shanqing Yu,Qi Xuan,Joey Tianyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces RAGP, a novel method for text prompt compression that uses graph-based structures to capture richer relationships between text elements, outperforming existing methods on various benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prompt compression is critical for optimizing natural language processing (NLP) tasks; however, current methods fail to capture the relational structure of information distributed throughout text.

Method: The authors propose Redundancy-Aware Graph Pruning (RAGP), utilizing a multiplex graph to model both fine-grained attention dependencies and coarse-grained semantic relations. They employ Levy walks for efficient identification of non-redundant nodes.

Result: RAGP achieves superior performance on the LongBench benchmark with an average score of 49.3 under a 4x compression ratio, outperforming LongLLMLingua, which scores 48.8 under a 3x ratio.

Conclusion: RAGP not only compresses text more effectively but also surpasses state-of-the-art methods across multiple tasks, proving its efficacy in capturing complex textual relationships.

Abstract: Existing prompt compression methods treat text as flat token sequences, failing to capture the distributed nature of important information, which is often spread across multiple locations and connected through both local syntactic dependencies and global semantic relations. Such relational structure is naturally represented as a graph, where tokens or sentences become nodes and their dependencies become edges. To this end, we propose RAGP, which formulates prompt compression as Redundancy-Aware Graph Pruning on a multiplex graph that jointly models fine-grained attention-based dependencies and coarse-grained semantic relations. To efficiently identify non-redundant nodes in this heterogeneous structure (dense local subgraphs and sparse global connections), we employ Levy walks whose heavy-tailed step distribution naturally balances local exploitation with global exploration. Experiments on LongBench show that RAGP achieves an average score of 49.3 under a 4x compression ratio, outperforming existing LLM-based compression methods, such as LongLLMLingua, which attains 48.8 at a 3x compression ratio. Besides, RAGP also surpasses state-of-the-art vision-based text compression paradigms on multiple tasks. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/RAGP-B0CB.

</details>


### [100] [Office Comprehension Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01245)
*Firoz Shaik,Mateus Picanço Lima Gomes,Tanvir Aumi,Jingci Wang,Milos Milunovic,Filip Basara,Ivana Jovanovic,Vishwas Suryanarayanan,Neha Nandan Kenkare,Weiyao Xie,Zhipeng Han,Zheng Zhang,Waleed Shahid,Jay Rathi,Russell Scherer,Thong Q. Nguyen,Michael Bentley,Tamara Stankovic,Rasika Chakravarthy,Vishal Chowdhary*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The Office Comprehension Bench (OCB) is introduced as the first public benchmark for evaluating LLM systems on comprehending Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of standardized benchmarks for assessing LLM systems' capabilities to understand and reason over complex office file formats and industry-specific documents.

Method: OCB includes two tracks: File Fidelity Q&A for structural and visual understanding of office elements, and Domain Q&A for expert-level reasoning on industry-specific real-world documents. Reference answers are broken down into claims and scored by an ensemble of LLM judges.

Result: Even the best LLM system in default reasoning mode achieves only 59.3% on Domain Q&A, and higher product tiers offer modest performance gains.

Conclusion: OCB highlights the challenge LLMs face in comprehending complex office files and industry-specific tasks, while providing tools for evaluation, a judge prompt, and a leaderboard to drive progress in this domain.

Abstract: We introduce Office Comprehension Bench (OCB), the first public benchmark to jointly evaluate LLM systems on Word, Excel, and PowerPoint comprehension over native file formats (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx) and their variants. OCB consists of two tracks. File Fidelity Q&A tests structural and visual perception of office artifacts - tables, charts, embedded images, formulas, and app-specific elements such as headers, speaker notes, and named ranges. Domain Q&A tests expert-level reasoning grounded in real-world industry documents across 12 professional domains, with queries requiring multi-step analysis and synthesis across documents. Each reference answer is decomposed into atomic, binary-gradable claims, and an ensemble of LLM judges scores responses against each claim independently. Even the strongest frontier system in its default reasoning mode reaches only about 59.3% on Domain Q&A; increasing thinking depth within a tier does not move performance materially, while moving to a higher product tier yields modest gains. We release the dataset, evaluation tooling, judge prompt, and a public leaderboard.

</details>


### [101] [RuleChef: Grounding LLM Task Knowledge in Human-Editable Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01293)
*Ádám Kovács,Nadia Verdha,Gábor Recski*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RuleChef uses LLMs to generate executable rules for NLP tasks like classification, NER, and relation extraction, iteratively improved via feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to provide a fast, deterministic, inspectable rule-based system for NLP tasks by leveraging LLMs to synthesize and refine rules.

Method: An iterative rule generation process using LLMs based on labeled examples, task descriptions, and human feedback. It can also bootstrap rules from observed input-output pairs.

Result: RuleChef yields executable rule systems for NLP tasks that are deterministic and inspectable, with preliminary evaluations on classification and NER.

Conclusion: RuleChef offers a novel way to use LLMs at learning time for rule generation, resulting in a functional, open-source framework for NLP tasks.

Abstract: We present RuleChef, a framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to generate executable rules for NLP tasks such as text classification, Named Entity Recognition (NER), or relation extraction. Rules are generated based on a task description and a set of labeled examples, then they are iteratively improved based both on additional examples and on human feedback overexisting rules. RuleChef can also be used to bootstrap rules using the observed input-output pairs from any existing model for a given task. LLMs are used only at learning time, synthesizing rules and iteratively patching them based on failures measured on a held-out split. The result of this process is a fast, deterministic, and inspectable rule system. Preliminary evaluation is performed on both classification and NER tasks. We release RuleChef as open-source software under an Apache 2.0

</details>


### [102] [TurnNat: Automatic Evaluation of Turn-Taking Naturalness in Dyadic Spoken Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01345)
*Hao Zhang,Thomas Thebaud,Georgi Tinchev,Venkatesh Ravichandran,Laureano Moro-Velazquez*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces TurnNat, a likelihood-based framework to automatically evaluate turn-taking naturalness in spoken dialogue, avoiding the reliance on human judgment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations for turn-taking naturalness in dialogue systems rely too much on human assessment or behavior-specific metrics, making comparisons across various timing issues difficult.

Method: The framework, TurnNat, uses a causal turn-taking prediction model trained on natural conversation. It calculates negative log-likelihood (NLL) scores from observed voice activities and aggregates these scores into dialogue-level naturalness metrics.

Result: Experiments with controlled dialogue perturbations validated by human judgments show the framework effectively detects unnatural behaviors caused by diverse timing failures.

Conclusion: TurnNat provides a unified, data-driven approach for evaluating turn-taking naturalness, addressing the challenges of traditional methods reliant on human and specific timing judgments.

Abstract: Turn-taking naturalness is central to full-duplex spoken dialogue systems, yet its automatic evaluation remains limited. Existing evaluations often rely on human judgments or behavior-specific timing metrics, making it difficult to compare heterogeneous timing failures within a unified framework. We propose TurnNat, a likelihood-based framework for automatic turn-taking naturalness evaluation in two-channel spoken dialogue. A causal turn-taking prediction model trained on natural conversations estimates future two-speaker voice-activity states, and the negative log-likelihood (NLL) of the observed future activity measures timing atypicality. TurnNat pools frame-level NLLs over turn-taking boundary units (TBUs) extracted from utterance onsets and offsets, and aggregates mean and tail TBU scores into a dialogue-level naturalness score. We further construct a controlled perturbation benchmark of paired natural and perturbed dialogue clips, validated by human naturalness judgments. Experiments on this benchmark show that TurnNat successfully identifies unnatural turn-taking perturbations across heterogeneous timing failures.

</details>


### [103] [RusFinChain: A Russian Benchmark for Verifiable Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in Finance with Fuzzy-Aligned Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01388)
*M. K. Arabov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RusFinChain introduces the first Russian-language symbolic benchmark for verifiable Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in finance. It evaluates reasoning with 5,280 examples and proposes new metrics, highlighting challenges in LLMs' reasoning abilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the lack of robust benchmarks for multi-step symbolic reasoning in finance, particularly focusing on the Russian language and intermediate reasoning steps.

Method: The authors developed RusFinChain, a benchmark consisting of executable Python templates for creating contamination-free financial reasoning tasks in Russian. It introduces gold-standard reasoning chains, numeric value verification, and enhanced evaluation metrics.

Result: Evaluating eight LLMs, the study found a significant reasoning gap with Hard F1 at ~0.65 for step alignment and only ~29% accuracy in final answers. New metrics showed better correlation with correctness, outperforming prior ChainEval metrics.

Conclusion: RusFinChain advances diagnostic capabilities for reasoning in Russian financial tasks but highlights key challenges for LLMs in achieving reliable multi-step reasoning. The dataset and tools are publicly available for further research.

Abstract: Multi-step symbolic reasoning is essential for robust financial analysis, yet most benchmarks neglect intermediate reasoning steps. FINCHAIN introduced verifiable Chain-of-Thought (CoT) evaluation but is limited to English. FINESSE-Bench includes a Russian block but relies on multiple-choice questions without step-level supervision. We present RusFinChain, the first Russian-language symbolic benchmark for verifiable CoT reasoning in finance. It spans 17 domains, 172 topics, and comprises 5,280 parameterized examples from executable Python templates, ensuring contamination-free evaluation. Each example includes a gold-standard reasoning chain with intermediate numeric values for automatic verification. We also introduce enhanced metrics: Fuzzy Numeric Alignment and Soft-Attention Alignment. We evaluate 8 open-weight LLMs on a stratified sample, generating 8,100 responses. Results reveal a substantial reasoning gap: models achieve Hard F1 of ~0.65 for step alignment, but only ~29% of final answers are correct. Our fuzzy and soft metrics show stronger correlation with final-answer correctness (Spearman rho approx 0.48) than the original ChainEval (rho approx 0.38-0.46), demonstrating superior diagnostic power. We release dataset, code, and evaluation framework to foster verifiable financial AI for the Russian-speaking community.

</details>


### [104] [Multi-Objective Exploration and Preference Optimization via Mutual Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01392)
*Hongyan Xie,Yikun Ban,Ruiyu Fang,Zixuang Huang,Deqing Wang,Jianxin Li,Shuangyong Song*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes MI-EPO, an information-theoretic framework to improve large language model alignment with diverse human values, achieving better controllability and multi-objective trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of aligning large language models with conflicting human values using multi-objective alignment techniques.

Method: The authors introduced MI-EPO, which leverages joint conditional mutual information and a probabilistic routing mechanism to improve multi-objective exploration and response alignment with preference vectors.

Result: Using MI-EPO, experiments demonstrated improved alignment of AI-generated responses with preference vectors, enhanced control over responses, and more stable multi-objective trade-offs.

Conclusion: MI-EPO effectively unifies exploration and alignment processes, offering a robust framework for improving the controllability and alignment of AI responses with diverse human preferences.

Abstract: Aligning large language models with diverse and heterogeneous human values requires multi-objective alignment methods to effectively trade off conflicting preference dimensions. Current methods achieve this trade-off by training policies conditioned on preference vectors and leveraging online direct preference optimization. However, exploration uncertainty can cause the reward distributions of responses generated under different preference vectors to overlap, and the generated responses may fail to effectively align with the corresponding preference vectors. In this paper, we propose Multi-Objective Exploration and Preference Optimization via Mutual Information (MI-EPO), an information-theoretic framework. It unifies multi-objective exploration and alignment by maximizing the joint conditional mutual information among generated responses, preference feedback, and preference vectors. By incorporating a probabilistic routing mechanism, MI-EPO naturally decomposes objective alignment and preference-aware exploration, encouraging the model to generate responses that are distinguishable and aligned with different preference conditions. Experiments on safe alignment and helpful assistant tasks show that MI-EPO significantly improves the alignment between generated responses and preference vectors, makes the outputs more controllable, and achieves stable trade-offs across multiple objectives.

</details>


### [105] [MultAttnAttrib: Training-Free Multimodal Attribution in Long Document Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01420)
*Dang Quang Thien Tran,Quang V. Dang,Vinamra Tyagi,Sai Soorya Rao Veeravalli,Trang Nguyen,Ryan A. Rossi,Franck Dernoncourt,Nedim Lipka,Koustava Goswami,Samyadeep Basu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel method, MultAttnAttrib, and an evaluation dataset, MultAttrEval, for enhancing and assessing attribution accuracy in QA systems handling multimodal documents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the under-researched challenge of attributing generated QA system answers accurately in the multimodal domain, which is critical for trust and safety.

Method: Proposed MultAttnAttrib, a training-free technique leveraging attention mechanisms to locate evidence in multimodal documents, alongside a benchmark dataset, MultAttrEval, annotated for such attribution.

Result: MultAttnAttrib outperforms prior methods and matches advanced models like GPT 5.4 in accuracy, while achieving significantly reduced latency.

Conclusion: MultAttnAttrib offers an effective, efficient solution to multimodal attribution, advancing the field in accuracy and model latency reduction.

Abstract: As grounded QA systems are increasingly deployed in AI assistants, accurately attributing generated answers to evidence is critical for user trust and model safety. While unimodal attributions have been explored in depth, the multimodal setting remains relatively under-researched. As a result, we introduce MultAttnAttrib, a training-free attribution-generation method that leverages a model's prefill pass, selected attention heads, and calibrated thresholds to locate source evidence within a document. To establish baseline results for the method, we introduce MultAttrEval, a complementary benchmark dataset annotated with fine-grained, ground-truth attributions for answer components grounded in multimodal source documents. To our knowledge, this is the first evaluation dataset designed specifically for multimodal attribution in long-form documents. Experimental results show that MultAttnAttrib consistently outperforms a variety of attribution-generation methods, including several strong prompting-based approaches and matches the latest frontier models such as GPT 5.4. Our method not only substantially improves attribution accuracy for both unimodal and multimodal attribution types, but also produces attributions at up to one-seventh of the direct inference latency compared to prompting on the same base model.

</details>


### [106] [IsoSci: A Benchmark of Isomorphic Cross-Domain Science Problems for Evaluating Reasoning versus Knowledge Retrieval in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01431)
*Samir Abdaljalil,Erchin Serpedin,Hasan Kurban*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ISOSCI is a benchmark for LLM evaluation that shows reasoning-mode gains are mostly knowledge-dependent. Results question chain-of-thought reasoning's enhancement of short-problem solving, with benchmark choice affecting utility conclusions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of separating reasoning ability from domain knowledge retrieval in LLM evaluations.

Method: Developed ISOSCI benchmark comprising isomorphic cross-domain pairs, tested reasoning-mode and knowledge dependency across various models.

Result: 91.3% of reasoning-mode gains are knowledge-dependent, reasoning toggles provide <5% accuracy gain, and model performance varies significantly by benchmark.

Conclusion: Benchmark design critically influences conclusions about reasoning utility in LLMs, challenging assumptions about chain-of-thought reasoning efficacy in specific tasks.

Abstract: We introduce ISOSCI, a benchmark of isomorphic cross-domain science problem pairs that separates reasoning ability from domain knowledge retrieval in LLM evaluation. Each pair shares identical logical structure but requires different domain-specific knowledge, enabling controlled attribution of reasoning-mode gains. Across five model pairs spanning four model families, we find that 91.3% of reasoning-mode gains are knowledge-dependent rather than structure-invariant (63/69 gains; Wilson 95% CI [82.3%, 96.0%]), directly challenging the assumption that chain-of-thought reasoning improves short-horizon procedural scientific problem-solving. Reasoning toggles on highly capable models provide less than 5 percentage points accuracy gain across all domains, and a reasoning-specialized model (o3-mini) that outperforms its standard counterpart on GPQA Diamond (+19.2 percentage points) underperforms on ISOSCI (-24.7 percentage points), showing that benchmark choice determines conclusions about reasoning utility. We release ISOSCI at https://huggingface.co/datasets/isosci/isosci

</details>


### [107] [FaithMed: Training LLMs For Faithful Evidence-Based Medical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01440)
*Zhiyun Zhang,Liwen Sun,Xiang Qian,Chenyan Xiong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: FaithMed, a framework combining clinician-designed rubrics and reinforcement learning, enhances reasoning faithfulness and reliability in medical decision-making by applying step-level process supervision, improving average rubric scores and task success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical large language models (LLMs) struggle to provide reliable, evidence-based reasoning for clinical decisions, either lacking access to evidence or failing to justify their use of retrieved evidence during reasoning.

Method: The authors formalize evidence-based medicine principles into process-level criteria and design FaithMed, which incorporates clinician-designed rubrics refined automatically and reinforcement learning with step-level process reward assignment alongside advantage grouping.

Result: FaithMed surpasses agentic-search baselines (+9% on average) and outcome-only reinforcement learning (+5.8%), achieving a significant +15.5% improvement in rubric scores on evidence-based medicine benchmarks.

Conclusion: Using explicit step-level supervision not only enhances task success but also ensures the trustworthiness and faithfulness of medical LLMs' reasoning processes, showcasing potential for broader clinical applications.

Abstract: Faithful reasoning is essential in medicine, where clinical decisions require transparent justification grounded in reliable evidence. Current medical LLMs either lack active access to evidence or use retrieved evidence without supervising how it should be appraised and applied during reasoning. To address this, we formalize evidence-based medicine principles as process-level criteria and introduce FaithMed, a framework that combines clinician-designed, automatically refined rubrics with reinforcement learning using step-level process reward assignment and advantage grouping. Across seven medical benchmarks, FaithMed improves over agentic-search baselines (+9% on average) and outcome-only RL (+5.8%), while raising average evidence-based medicine rubric scores over agentic-search Qwen3 baselines (+15.5%). This work demonstrates that explicit step-level supervision can improve both task success and the faithfulness of the reasoning process. Code is available at https://github.com/cxcscmu/FaithMed.

</details>


### [108] [Grounded Optimization: A Layered Engineering Framework for Reducing LLM Hallucination in Automated Personal Document Rewriting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01457)
*Shashank Indukuri,Adarsh Agrawal*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Grounded Optimization is a framework to reduce hallucination failures in large language models when applied to resume optimization for applicant tracking systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are prone to introducing specific types of hallucination errors in resume optimization that compromise their reliability.

Method: The paper proposes a five-layer framework, including temporal context validation, deterministic contamination detection, structural invariant enforcement, prompt-level grounding, and an evaluator agent.

Result: Their method significantly reduces hallucination errors, achieving near-zero rates under ideal conditions with capable models.

Conclusion: By combining multiple layered defenses, hallucination in resume optimization by LLMs can be effectively minimized. Prompt-level grounding offers a strong base, but deterministic defenses are necessary for higher temperatures and weaker models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to resume optimization for applicant tracking systems, introducing hallucination failures distinct from general text generation: anachronistic technology injection, cross-domain terminology contamination, structural mutation, and content fabrication. We present Grounded Optimization, a five-layer framework combining temporal context validation, deterministic contamination detection, structural invariant enforcement, prompt-level grounding, and an evaluator agent.
  In ablation experiments across three LLMs, four temperature settings, and six layer configurations on 25 synthetic resumes spanning 14 industries, undefended baselines produce 2.48-5.36 detected hallucinations per resume. Among detectors independent of the active defenses, temporal hallucinations are reduced by 50-95% across all conditions; overall detected hallucination rate falls to 0.04-0.24. Prompt-level grounding alone achieves zero detected hallucinations at low temperature with a capable instruction-following model; higher temperatures and weaker models reveal the need for the deterministic layers as a complement. We release the contamination taxonomy, evaluation code, and raw data.

</details>


### [109] [Comparing Architectures for Supervised Political Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01464)
*Anna Golub,Sebastian Padó*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper discusses improving text scaling methods in political analysis by exploring joint prediction of scales and a middle ground between classification and regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of existing NLP methods in positioning political actors on ideological scales and improve their performance.

Method: The authors investigate joint prediction of scales and explore a compromise between classification-based and regression-based approaches.

Result: Results highlight potential advancements in scaling methods by integrating both prediction strategies.

Conclusion: The paper suggests that combining joint prediction with a hybrid classification-regression approach can enhance the effectiveness of text scaling techniques in political analysis.

Abstract: Text scaling, the task of positioning political actors on an ideological scale, is a fundamental task in political analysis. To ease the need for manual analysis, various NLP methods have been proposed for this task, including classification- and regression-based approaches, showing successes as well as limitations. The goal of our paper is to consolidate the state of the art in this area. We ask two questions: (a) Can the performance of scaling methods be improved by predicting scales not individually but jointly? (b) Is there a middle ground between classification and regression?

</details>


### [110] [From Monolingual to Multilingual: Evaluating Mamba for ASR in South African Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01502)
*Jesujoba O. Alabi,Julian Herreilers,Badr M. Abdullah,Dietrich Klakow*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the Mamba speech recognition model on seven South African languages. It shows Mamba competes with Conformer models in accuracy while using fewer resources and training faster. Multilingual training improves performance, and adding explicit language information aids robustness but not in-domain accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the performance and generalization ability of state-of-the-art speech recognition models, particularly Mamba, on underexplored African languages, and to evaluate strategies for multilingual low-resource ASR settings.

Method: The study conducts both monolingual and multilingual ASR experiments. It evaluates Mamba against Conformer models in terms of accuracy and resource efficiency. Multilingual experiments include pooling languages, adding language-family embeddings, and multitask learning with CTC and LID objectives. Ablation studies focus on low-resource settings with limited training data.

Result: Mamba achieves comparable accuracy to the Conformer model with reduced computational complexity and faster training. Multilingual training enhances performance over monolingual setups, while explicit language embeddings improve robustness across corpora but show no significant in-domain gains. Language embeddings serve as task-specific control vectors rather than capturing linguistic typologies.

Conclusion: The Mamba model is a resource-efficient and effective choice for ASR in African languages, particularly in low-resource settings. Multilingual training benefits performance, and language-specific embeddings provide robustness, though their role is more functional than linguistic.

Abstract: Recent advances in automatic speech recognition (ASR) have explored different sequence models, including Conformer-based models and newer state space models such as Mamba. Although prior work has evaluated these architectures in multiple languages, their effectiveness in African languages remains underexplored. In this work, we evaluate Mamba for ASR on seven South African languages. In monolingual experiments, each model is trained on 50 hours of speech per language, and we compare Mamba to a Conformer baseline of similar parameter scale. Mamba achieves similar recognition accuracy to Conformer while using fewer computational resources and training faster. We further evaluate generalization in this setting and find that both models struggle to generalize to speech that is much longer than what they were trained on. We then study multilingual ASR using Mamba models, where the baseline is pooling all languages together. On top of this, we tested three extensions: training with language-family information by adding both language and language-family embeddings as biases to the downsampled acoustic representations, and multitask learning with a CTC ASR objective and a language identification (LID) head. We find that multilingual training consistently improves performance over monolingual training. However, adding explicit language information does not improve in-domain performance but does improve cross-corpus robustness. We conducted ablation studies in low-resource multilingual settings using 5-hour and 10-hour per-language training data, where we observed gains from using language embeddings and further demonstrated that removing or altering them hurt model performance. Lastly, we analysed these embeddings and find that they do not capture linguistic similarity in a typological sense, but instead act as task-specific control vectors.

</details>


### [111] [Parameter Golf: What Really Works?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01517)
*Prashanna Mani Paudel,Shivanand Venkanna Sheshappanavar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper studies how much a language model can improve under a limited artifact budget, focusing on a community challenge, and demonstrates a 13.6% reduction in bits-per-byte (BPB).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research aims to understand the optimization limits of language models under strict constraints, exploring how improvements can be made with limited storage, computational time, and resources.

Method: The study analyzes data from a community contest, evaluating 84 optimization techniques across 2,037 pull requests and 1,430 submissions. It measures their individual and combined impact on the BPB metric.

Result: The contest led to a 13.6% reduction in BPB, decreasing from 1.2244 to 1.058 BPB. Most techniques provided only incremental improvements, often less than 1%.

Conclusion: The findings highlight the diminishing returns of individual optimization techniques under competitive constraints and isolate a few key strategies that consistently enhance model performance across different setups.

Abstract: How far can a language model improve under a strict artifact budget? Parameter Golf posed this question as an open community challenge in which participants trained the best language model, with the complete artifact (training code + compressed weights) required to fit within 16 MB and be trained in under ten minutes on 8xH100 SXM GPUs. Quality was measured in bits-per-byte (BPB), the average number of bits required to encode each byte of unseen text. We analyze 2,037 pull requests and 1,430 clean scored submissions from the contest, build a taxonomy of 84 optimization techniques, and measure each technique's contribution to BPB. The verified leaderboard score dropped from 1.2244 to 1.058 BPB across three phases -- a 13.6% reduction, despite individual techniques rarely improving BPB by more than 1%. We show that most gains in techniques shrink across competitive submissions, isolating the few methods that improve performance across stacks.

</details>


### [112] [Can Language Models Actually Retrieve In-Context? Drowning in Documents at Million Token Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01538)
*Siddharth Gollapudi,Nilesh Gupta,Prasann Singhal,Sewon Min*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces BlockSearch, a language model for in-context corpus-scale retrieval, achieving performance parity with dense retrieval methods while addressing challenges of lengthy contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore language models' potential in corpus-scale retrieval tasks and overcome challenges in length generalization beyond training sizes.

Method: Introduced BlockSearch, a novel LM retriever with architectural and training modifications, leveraging length-aware attention adjustments to improve corpus-scale retrieval.

Result: BlockSearch matches dense retrieval performance on benchmarks, outperforming MSA and excelling at tasks requiring unique similarity notions, showcasing robustness at extreme context growth.

Conclusion: In-context retrieval via LMs is a viable alternative to traditional methods, but controlling attention under large-scale contexts remains a critical challenge to address.

Abstract: Language models (LMs) raise an intriguing alternative to vector-based retrieval: conditioning on an in-context corpus and directly generating a relevant answer. However, prior work has largely focused on proprietary systems or the smaller-scale reranking task, leaving corpus-scale in-context retrieval largely unexplored. In this work, we present the first systematic study of in-context retrieval on two scales practical retrievers demand: million-token corpora and length-generalization far beyond training-time sizes. We first introduce BlockSearch, a 0.6B LM retriever whose architectural and training modifications improve over prior LM baselines and length-generalize up to 10 times beyond its training regime. Nevertheless, retrieval still collapses under more extreme extrapolation. We trace this failure to an attention dilution effect: as the corpus grows, irrelevant documents dominate the softmax denominator, reducing the normalized mass on the gold document even when its pre-softmax score stays high. Motivated by this analysis, we introduce length-aware adjustments to the attention softmax and document-level sparse attention. With these modifications, at the million-token scale, our model matches dense retrieval on widely studied benchmarks (e.g, MS MARCO and NQ), while outperforming the concurrent model MSA despite being 7 times smaller. Furthermore, it significantly outperforms dense retrieval on tasks requiring entirely different notions of similarity, such as LIMIT, achieving a 3 times higher score. Together, our results position in-context retrieval a promising alternative to classical retrieval while emphasizing attention control under extreme context growth as a new challenge.

</details>


### [113] [DiPS: Dialogue Policy Selection for High-Stakes Persuasion Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01557)
*Tianyi Zhang,Mousumi Das,Abrar Anwar,Jesse Thomason,David Traum*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces DiPS, a Q-learning framework designed to improve persuasion by dynamically adapting dialogue policies in high-stakes scenarios, significantly outperforming existing LLM-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of large language models (LLMs) in effectively persuading individuals in high-stakes scenarios that require personalized and context-sensitive strategies.

Method: The authors propose Dialogue Policy Selection (DiPS), which employs a Q-learning framework where a trained critic selects the optimal persuasion strategy at each dialogue turn based on the conversational context.

Result: DiPS outperforms zero-shot LLMs and RAG-augmented approaches in simulated and real interactions, achieving higher success in persuading individuals to evacuate in a fire-rescue scenario.

Conclusion: The study concludes that dynamically tailored dialogue strategies, enabled by frameworks like DiPS, are effective in improving persuasive communication in critical, high-stakes scenarios.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) often struggle with persuasion in high-stakes scenarios. People's individual personalities and concerns require tailored strategies rather than a one-size-fits-all approach. To address this challenge, we focus on a fire-rescue scenario in which an operator must persuade a resident to evacuate as a high-stakes persuasion domain and propose Dialogue Policy Selection (DiPS), a Q-learning framework to dynamically select persuasion strategies adapted to the evolving conversational context. Specifically, we train a critic, trained to maximize the chance of evacuation success, to select a persuasion policy at each turn based on the resident's recent utterances.We then evaluate DiPS against multiple baselines in both simulated and real human interactions. We find that DiPS achieves higher evacuation success than a zero-shot LLM and generic RAG-augmented approach.

</details>


### [114] [Beyond Skepticism: Evaluating LLMs Pedagogical Intent Reasoning with the Adaptive Pedagogical Vigilance Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01581)
*Minghao Chen,Ruihan Zhou,Jiayi Tang,Zihan Xu,Bowen Huang,Yuxin Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Adaptive Pedagogical Vigilance (APV) framework to improve Large Language Models' (LLMs) reasoning about pedagogical intent through Bayesian modeling, achieving robust performance in pedagogical settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the unexplored potential of LLMs in understanding pedagogical intent, particularly in educational contexts like translation pedagogy.

Method: Proposes the APV framework utilizing a Bayesian Pedagogical Intent Inference Engine (PIIE) to model instructional scenarios and evaluate LLMs' vigilance across instructional genres, structured setups, and real-world discourse.

Result: Experiments with LLMs like GPT-4 and Claude 3.5 show that APV significantly enhances model vigilance, aligns highly with human judgments ($r=0.958$), and maintains robustness where other models falter.

Conclusion: APV establishes a unified framework for improving LLMs’ understanding of pedagogical motives, contributing to the advancement of AI-assisted education.

Abstract: The capacity of Large Language Models (LLMs) to reason about pedagogical intent within instructional communication remains underexplored, particularly in educational domains such as translation pedagogy. To address this, we propose the \textbf{Adaptive Pedagogical Vigilance (APV)} framework, a novel computational formalism that reframes communicative vigilance as an adaptive mechanism for optimizing learning through intent inference. APV formalizes the problem via a Bayesian Pedagogical Intent Inference Engine (PIIE), which models how instructors select content to maximize pedagogical utility and how vigilant learners should inversely reason about latent instructional configurations -- encompassing genre, stance, and incentives. We evaluate APV through a three-tier hierarchy: distinguishing instructional genre, reasoning about structured pedagogical setups, and generalizing to authentic educational discourse. Experiments on leading LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, Claude 3.5) show that APV substantially improves model vigilance. It achieves the strongest discrimination between pedagogical and exposure-based content, correlates highly with human judgments ($r=0.958$), and maintains robust performance on naturalistic data where baseline methods degrade. This work establishes a unified framework for assessing and enhancing LLMs' understanding of pedagogical motives, advancing the development of more reliable AI-assisted learning systems.

</details>


### [115] [ProWAFT: A ROMA-LPD Instance for Workload-Aware and Dynamic Fault Tolerance in FPGA-Based CNN Accelerators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01602)
*Xinxin Chen,Haoran Qiao,Yiming Guo,Kecheng Luo,Siyuan Feng,Jingwen Ma*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ProWAFT is a fault-tolerance framework for FPGA-based CNN accelerators that selectively applies TMR through partial reconfiguration, minimizing latency, energy, and reliability trade-offs while ensuring high performance and task success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SRAM-based FPGAs face reliability challenges due to transient faults during CNN inference, necessitating efficient fault-tolerance strategies that balance redundancy, latency, and energy.

Method: ProWAFT employs proactive, workload-aware strategies using partial reconfiguration to selectively apply TMR in reconfigurable regions, optimizing for latency, energy, and reliability.

Result: ProWAFT, tested on the Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ ZCU104 platform with ResNet-18, MobileNetV2, and EfficientNet-Lite, outperformed static TMR and reactive approaches, achieving high task success, low composite cost, and minimal decision overhead.

Conclusion: ProWAFT offers a reliable and efficient solution for CNN inference on FPGAs, balancing fault-tolerance with energy and latency constraints while maintaining strong performance and reliability.

Abstract: SRAM-based FPGAs provide an attractive platform for energy- and latency-constrained CNN inference at the network edge, yet transient faults can lead to silent errors that compromise reliability. Always-on redundancy (e.g., full TMR) improves correctness but incurs substantial performance and energy overhead, while reactive recovery may introduce unacceptable latency on the critical path. We propose \textbf{ProWAFT}, a proactive workload-aware fault-tolerance framework for FPGA-based CNN accelerators that uses partial reconfiguration to selectively apply TMR across reconfigurable partitions. ProWAFT quantifies workload criticality, models fault propagation and reconfiguration overhead, and selects configurations that minimize a composite objective over latency, energy, and reliability risk. Implemented on a Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ ZCU104 platform with six reconfigurable regions and evaluated on a 500-task trace derived from ResNet-18, MobileNetV2, and EfficientNet-Lite under time-varying SEU injection, ProWAFT achieves lower composite cost than static TMR and reactive reconfiguration while maintaining high task success rate and near-baseline throughput with low online decision overhead.

</details>


### [116] [When Does Generating More Help? Disentangling Fixed-Source Synthesis from Source Expansion in Synthetic Data Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01727)
*Xu Guo,Jian Tong,Zhihui Lu,Qipeng Guo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores Fixed-Source Synthesis (FSS), where synthetic data is scaled by increasing the response budget for a fixed data source, contrasting it with Source Expansion (SE).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To examine the underexplored area of FSS by isolating its effects on data synthesis and comparing it with SE at different scaling budgets.

Method: The authors isolate FSS by keeping seed questions and teacher models constant while varying the per-question response budget under Rejection Sampling. They derive a rectified scaling law for FSS and empirically evaluate it.

Result: The rectified scaling law accurately predicts performance under FSS; SE surpasses FSS at large budgets, while plain Rejection Sampling is consistently effective within FSS.

Conclusion: FSS offers a constrained framework for studies and is capped in scalability. The study shows the potential superiority of SE over FSS at higher budgets and questions the utility of alternative synthesis protocols within FSS.

Abstract: Synthetic data can be scaled along two routes: Source Expansion (SE), which enlarges the source by adding seed materials or generators, and Fixed-Source Synthesis (FSS), which holds the source fixed and scales the generation budget. Existing scaling studies typically expand the source as the data grows, conflating SE with FSS and leaving FSS underexplored. We isolate FSS by holding the seed-question pool and teacher model fixed, varying only the per-question response budget under Rejection Sampling (RS). We adapt the rectified scaling law to FSS, deriving it from how repeated sampling covers a fixed source. Empirically, the derived form, fit on low budgets, predicts performance at the held-out highest budget for every evaluated teacher--student pair. At matched total-sample budgets, SE and FSS are comparable at small budgets; at large budgets, adding seed questions outperforms spending the same budget on more responses. Within FSS, however, neither synthesizing additional questions from the existing seeds nor varying the synthesis protocol outperforms plain RS at matched budgets. FSS is thus a bounded scaling axis and a controlled setting for comparing synthesis protocols. We will release our code and data to facilitate further research.

</details>


### [117] [Rethinking Speech-LLM Integration for ASR: Effective Joint Speech-Text Training by Interleaving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01733)
*Ruchao Fan,Yiming Wang,Rui Zhao,Liliang Ren,Keqi Deng,Xiaoyang Chen,Ali Zare,Bo Ren,Yuxuan Hu,Junkun Chen,Yan Huang,Yelong Shen,Jinyu Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces JSTIP, a pretraining method integrating speech and text inputs to enhance ASR performance, specifically excelling in medical entity recognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the underutilization of textual knowledge in ASR tasks and to improve speech-LLM integration benefits, especially for large-scale supervised ASR setups.

Method: Proposed Joint Speech-Text Interleaved Pretraining (JSTIP), constructing interleaved speech-text sequences for speech-LLM models that handle continuous inputs.

Result: JSTIP outperforms baseline ASR and joint training methods in entity accuracy using 38k hours of ASR data. It simplifies domain adaptation by achieving strong performance with domain transcription text and supports zero-shot speech question answering.

Conclusion: The interleaving approach reduces the gap between speech and text modalities while preserving LLM's generative abilities, making JSTIP effective for ASR and domain-specific tasks such as medical entity recognition.

Abstract: Speech-LLM integration has shown promising results by leveraging extensive textual pretraining, yet its specific benefits for automatic speech recognition (ASR) remain unclear. We observe that as supervised ASR training data increases, the contribution of LLM priors becomes less evident, and simple speech-text joint training under-utilizes textual knowledge. We therefore propose Joint Speech-Text Interleaved Pretraining (JSTIP), an ASR-oriented pretraining strategy that constructs word-level and segment-level interleaved speech-text sequences within aligned pairs for speech-LLM architectures that accept continuous inputs. Experiments on 38k hours of ASR data show consistent entity accuracy improvement compared to ASR-only and joint speech-text training baselines. JSTIP achieves on-par entity recognition performance using domain transcription text compared to synthetic speech-text pairs, simplifying domain adaptation. Benefiting from textual pretraining and domain text data, JSTIP is competitive with open-source ASR and Speech-LLM systems in medical entity recognition. The zero-shot speech question answering behaviors further suggest that interleaving reduces the speech-text modality gap and preserves the LLM generative prior, which is likely the reason for the entity improvements on the ASR task.

</details>


### [118] [PARTREP: Learning What to Repeat for Decoder-only LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01792)
*Andikawati P Widjaja,Yongjun Kim,Hyounghun Kim,Jaeho Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces PartRep, a token selection method to optimize prompt repetition in decoder-only LLMs for better reasoning, while reducing computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decoder-only LLMs encounter limitations due to asymmetric contextual information flow, with later tokens being richer in grounding. Full prompt repetition improves reasoning but is computationally expensive for long-context settings.

Method: The proposed method, PartRep, selectively augments prompts by appending only high-negative log-likelihood tokens that are less predictable and benefit more from repetition. A lightweight gating model predicts high-NLL tokens during mid-prefill for efficient selection.

Result: PartRep achieved similar reasoning improvements as full prompt repetition, while using 59.4% less KV cache and 79.0% of prefill computation costs across eight benchmarks and three model families.
