Conclusion: By leveraging object masks for stabilization and extending the original anti-collapse objective from whole images to object sets, the proposed approach shows improved performance and data efficiency.

Abstract: Image encoders trained with LeJEPA can deliver strong features for downstream tasks, but, like other image-level self-supervised methods, typically require large training datasets. Aligning representations at the level of objects rather than whole scenes promises greater data efficiency, but doing this in a completely self-supervised way, effectively jointly partitioning a scene and representing its objects, is unstable: the two are locked in a cyclic dependency, partitioning requires meaningful representations, while meaningful representations require consistent partitioning. We sidestep this instability by taking object masks as given during training, using cheap, off-the-shelf SAM proposals. We extend LeJEPA - whose distributional anti-collapse objective ports naturally from whole images to variable-sized sets of objects - to align object-centric representations rather than whole images. An additional instance-separating loss, which treats other objects in the same scene as negatives, further boosts downstream performance. Across two model scales and 10-100% of COCO, object-level LeJEPA outperforms image-level LeJEPA on tracking (DAVIS), classification (ImageNet-1k), segmentation (ADE20k), and re-identification (NAVI).

</details>


### [262] [Wavelet-Guided Semantic Signal Compensation for Inversion-Free Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02421)
*Anqi Tang,Wenhao Sun,Zhaoqiang Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a frequency-aware semantic compensation strategy to improve text-guided image editing by addressing challenges in global attribute shifts, ensuring better edits while preserving the background.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations in text-guided image editing frameworks like FlowEdit, which struggle with effective global modifications due to high-noise regimes that reduce the impact of text-conditioned directions.

Method: A new frequency-aware semantic compensation strategy is proposed, which enhances effective signal strength during early generation stages while maintaining background consistency.

Result: The proposed method improves global editing abilities of image editing systems without compromising the fidelity of the background.

Conclusion: The approach effectively addresses prior limitations, providing a solution for better semantic modifications in image editing while preserving structural consistency.

Abstract: Text-guided image editing aims to modify visual content according to a target prompt while preserving the background. Recent inversion-free image editing frameworks such as FlowEdit have demonstrated strong editing capability without requiring inversion. Empirically, FlowEdit can achieve substantial semantic changes under appropriate hyperparameter settings. However, we observe that under certain global attribute shifts, the editing trajectory may not effectively move away from the source distribution in the early timesteps. Our analysis suggests that in the high-noise regime, the dominant manifold-seeking flow toward the data manifold can reduce the influence of the text-conditioned direction, leading to limited global modification while background structures remain only moderately preserved. Inspired by this observation, we propose an inversion-free, frequency-aware semantic compensation strategy that strengthens the effective signal in the early stage of generation, while maintaining structural consistency in the background. The proposed method improves global editing capacity without sacrificing background fidelity.

</details>


### [263] [Learning to Evolve Scenes: Reasoning about Human Activities with Scene Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02425)
*Francesca Pistilli,Simone Alberto Peirone,Giuseppe Averta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces SG-Ego, a spatio-temporal scene graph annotation set for Ego4D, and GLEN, a graph-based model for reasoning over these graphs to better understand human-environment interactions in first-person videos.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to improve understanding of human-environment interactions in first-person videos to enable structured reasoning over dynamic scenes instead of relying solely on implicit representations.

Method: They develop SG-Ego, a spatio-temporal scene graph dataset, and propose GLEN, a graph-based model that operates over these graphs to align them with textual actions and predict temporal scene evolution.

Result: GLEN demonstrates strong performance in downstream tasks such as retrieval and long-horizon reasoning benchmarks, outperforming raw video baselines and excelling in structured reasoning about scene changes.

Conclusion: Spatio-temporal scene graphs and models reasoning over them offer a robust, interpretable framework for video understanding, providing structured and editable predictions of scene dynamics driven by human activities.

Abstract: Understanding human behavior while interacting with the surrounding world is crucial for many applications of embodied AI. First-person videos are particularly informative for this problem, as they well capture how activities reshape the scene over time. However, existing approaches often rely on implicit visual or language-aligned representations, disregarding structured reasoning over the scene dynamic. We argue that explicit, compositional and editable representations of human-environment interactions can play a crucial role for rich grounded activity understanding. To this end, we introduce SG-Ego, a large scale annotation set extending Ego4D with spatio-temporal scene graphs, where relations triplets are consolidated over time into explicit time-evolving descriptions of the scene state. To reason over this representation, we propose GLEN, a graph-based model that operates over scene graph sequences to both align them with textual actions and model their temporal evolution. In addition, we formulate the activity-driven graph-edit forecasting (A-GEF) problem, a novel task that casts scene dynamics as a sequence of structured transformations conditioned on ongoing actions, enabling explicit reasoning about how scenes change over time. We validate our approach across multiple downstream tasks, spanning retrieval benchmarks as EgoMCQ and EgoCVR, as well as long-horizon reasoning benchmarks as EXPLORE-Bench and the newly introduced A-GEF. GLEN achieves strong results compared to raw video baselines and it excels in reasoning settings, typically addressed only with MLLMs, while enabling controllable and structured predictions of scene dynamics driven by human activities. We believe our results establish spatio-temporal scene graphs, together with models that reason over them, as strong compositional and interpretable representations for video understanding and potentially beyond.

</details>


### [264] [MARVEL: Margin-Aware Robust von Mises-Fischer Expert Learning for Long-Tailed Out-of-Distribution Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02435)
*A. S. Anudeep,Vaanathi Sundaresan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel framework to improve Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection in medical diagnostic systems using a Nonlinear von Mises-Fisher (NvMF) classifier, a multi-expert mechanism, and an outlier expert.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the unreliability of automated diagnostic systems when faced with OOD inputs, especially in the context of real-world, imbalanced, and clinically relevant medical datasets.

Method: The method includes a Nonlinear von Mises-Fisher classifier for flexible decision boundaries, a multi-expert framework for handling label imbalance, and an outlier expert to distinguish unfamiliar samples more effectively.

Result: The proposed methodology achieves significant FPR95 reductions (8.45%, 13.02%, and 36.90%) across RFMiD, ISIC2019, and NCTCRC datasets compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The framework improves OOD detection reliability, facilitating safer AI-assisted diagnosis, especially in clinical workflows. Comprehensive ablations validate the efficacy of each component.

Abstract: For clinical deployment, it is essential that automated diagnostic systems remain reliable when confronted with previously unseen cases, yet deep models routinely misclassify out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs with high confidence, underscoring the need for more robust OOD detection methods. Although substantial effort has been devoted to improving model robustness, most of the existing literature assumes balanced datasets, evaluates OOD detection on coarse or non-clinical OOD sources, or lacks comprehensive assessment across diverse OOD scenarios. To address the gaps, we propose a novel methodology trained on diverse and imbalanced medical datasets and evaluated across a clinically reflective OOD spectrum. Our framework comprises three key components: (1) a Nonlinear von Mises-Fisher (NvMF) classifier capable of learning non-linear decision boundaries, with theoretical proof of its asymptotic connection to cosine classifiers; (2) a multi-expert framework in which margin-aware NvMF classifiers specialise in different regions of label distribution to better handle imbalance; and (3) an outlier expert trained explicitly to distinguish inlier from outlier data, thereby strengthening OOD detection. Evaluation on RFMiD, ISIC2019, and NCTCRC datasets demonstrates consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods, achieving mean FPR95 reductions of 8.45%, 13.02%, and 36.90% respectively. These gains are further supported by comprehensive ablations that validated the contributions of each component. This enables reliable identification of unfamiliar cases for deferral to clinicians, supporting safer AI-assisted diagnosis in real-world workflows. Our code is available at https://github.com/redboxup/MARVEL.

</details>


### [265] [OrbitQuant: Data-Agnostic Quantization for Image and Video Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02461)
*Donghyun Lee,Jitesh Chavan,Duy Nguyen,Sam Huang,Liming Jiang,Priyadarshini Panda,Timo Mertens,Saurabh Shukla*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OrbitQuant introduces data-agnostic quantization for diffusion transformers by using a normalized, rotated basis, enabling efficient inference across diverse timesteps, prompts, and modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion transformers face expensive inference due to multi-step sampling and large parameter counts, highlighting the need for resource-efficient quantization techniques.

Method: OrbitQuant employs a randomized permuted block-Hadamard (RPBH) rotation to normalize and simplify activation quantization, using one generalized codebook. Weights are pre-rotated offline, simplifying runtime computations to a forward rotation.

Result: OrbitQuant achieves state-of-the-art post-training quantization (PTQ) at various low-bit settings, maintaining generation quality across image and video tasks without per-modality tuning.

Conclusion: OrbitQuant effectively streamlines PTQ for diffusion transformers, reducing computational overhead and extending applicability from images to videos without compromising quality.

Abstract: Diffusion transformers (DiTs) achieve state-of-the-art image and video generation, but their multi-step sampling and growing parameter count make inference expensive. Post-training quantization (PTQ) is the natural remedy, yet DiT activations shift across timesteps, prompts, and guidance branches, forcing prior methods to re-fit calibration data for every new checkpoint or modality. We present OrbitQuant, a data-agnostic weight-activation quantizer that bypasses range estimation by quantizing in a normalized, rotated basis. In this basis, a randomized permuted block-Hadamard (RPBH) rotation concentrates each coordinate around one fixed, known marginal regardless of the input, so a single Lloyd-Max codebook serves all timesteps, prompts, and layers of a given input dimension. We extend the same quantizer to weight rows offline, absorbing the rotation into the weights so that it cancels inside each linear layer and only a forward rotation on the activations remains at runtime. The same recipe transfers from image to video with no per-modality tuning. Across FLUX.1, Z-Image-Turbo, Wan 2.1, and CogVideoX, it sets the state of the art for PTQ at several low-bit settings. It also pushes PTQ of image diffusion transformers to W2A4 with usable generation quality.

</details>


### [266] [Interpretation-Oriented Cloud Removal via Observation-Anchored Residual Flow with Geo-Contextual Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02471)
*Ziyao Wang,Maonan Wang,Yucheng He,Xianping Ma,Ziyi Wang,Hongyang Zhang,Yirong Cheng,Man-on Pun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Geo-Anchored Cloud Removal (GACR) ensures both high-quality cloud removal and improved accuracy in remote sensing tasks through its innovative dual approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for cloud removal in remote sensing prioritize visual realism but neglect their impact on tasks like semantic segmentation and change detection. This can lead to semantic drift and poor downstream task performance, creating the need for a method that balances both faithful reconstruction and robust interpretability.

Method: GACR includes two key innovations: (1) Observation-Anchored Residual Flow (OAR-Flow) for accurate and physically grounded reconstruction by anchoring the generative process to input observations, and (2) Geo-Contextual Prior Alignment (GCPA) to preserve semantic structures using a Vision Foundation Model (VFM). This combination ensures spatial-semantic integrity.

Result: Experiments on six cloud removal datasets and twelve downstream remote sensing tasks show that GACR outperforms existing methods in reconstruction quality and consistently enhances downstream task accuracy.

Conclusion: GACR provides a unified framework to reliably remove clouds while maintaining semantic and spatial integrity, proving beneficial for various downstream remote sensing applications.

Abstract: Cloud removal (CR) is essential for optical remote sensing, serving as a prerequisite for reliable downstream interpretation, such as semantic segmentation and change detection. However, existing CR approaches often prioritize visual realism while overlooking their impact on subsequent analytical tasks, leading to semantic drift and degraded downstream performance. To address this issue, we propose Geo-Anchored Cloud Removal (GACR), a unified framework that jointly ensures faithful reconstruction and robust interpretability. At its core, GACR incorporates Observation-Anchored Residual Flow (OAR-Flow), which reformulates CR as a physically grounded residual inversion process. By anchoring the generative trajectory to the cloudy observation rather than pure noise, OAR-Flow enables fast, stable, and faithful reconstruction. To further preserve semantic structures critical for downstream interpretation, GACR integrates Geo-Contextual Prior Alignment (GCPA) to constrain the reconstruction within a semantic manifold induced by a Vision Foundation Model (VFM). Consequently, GACR strictly maintains the spatial-semantic integrity of complex landscapes. Extensive experiments across six CR datasets and twelve downstream tasks demonstrate that GACR produces superior reconstruction quality while consistently improving downstream task accuracy. The code is available at https://github.com/wzy6055/GACR.

</details>


### [267] [EAGLE-360: Embodied Active Global-to-Local Exploration in 360$^\circ$](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02479)
*Jingtao Xu,Zizhuo Lin,Jianwen Sun,Yi Yang,Yawei Luo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EAGLE-360 addresses difficulties faced by Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) when performing panoramic visual search tasks by integrating global priors and a novel positional encoding, achieving superior efficiency and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs struggle with polar distortion and cylindrical properties of panoramic views, degrading target detection accuracy. Current methods relying on fragmented local search suffer from inefficiency and poor error recovery.

Method: EAGLE-360 combines a global-to-local exploration framework with RoPE Rolling positional encoding to model panoramas' continuous topologies, training on the EAGLE-360 dataset and using SFT with GRPO for spatial reasoning and tool-calling.

Result: EAGLE-360 delivers an 8-fold increase in accuracy over base models and significantly improves exploration efficiency.

Conclusion: The framework resolves limitations in panoramic active search by leveraging global priors, iterative reasoning, and effective encoding, establishing a new standard for performance in 360° visual tasks.

Abstract: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated exceptional capabilities in standard visual understanding, adapting them for active visual search in 360$^\circ$ panoramic environments exposes fundamental limitations. Specifically, standard MLLMs struggle to effectively model inherent panoramic properties, such as severe polar distortion and continuous cylindrical topologies, which significantly degrades target detection accuracy. Consequently, existing panoramic search methods attempt to compensate by relying heavily on fragmented local viewpoints. Burdened by rigid initialization and a lack of global panoramic priors, these approaches suffer from myopic, inefficient exploration and struggle with robust error recovery when targets are out of view. To overcome these challenges, we propose EAGLE-360, a novel Embodied Active Global-to-Local Exploration framework. Rather than performing exhaustive local searches, EAGLE-360 leverages global priors to establish an initial holistic perspective, iteratively reasoning and progressively narrowing the search space. Architecturally, we adapt RoPE Rolling, a coordinate-shifting positional encoding mechanism, to seamlessly model the continuous topologies of panoramas. To facilitate this paradigm, we construct the large-scale EAGLE-360 dataset, comprising 14,000+ 4K panoramas and 70,000+ rounds of high-quality VQA dialogues. By employing a training pipeline that integrates Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), we effectively elicit complex spatial reasoning and tool-calling capabilities. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EAGLE-360 establishes a new state-of-the-art for 360$^\circ$ visual search, achieving nearly an 8-fold increase in accuracy over the base model while significantly enhancing exploration efficiency.

</details>


### [268] [Combating Textual Noise and Redundancy: Entropy-Aware Dense Visual Token Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02484)
*Xuehui Wang,Xuankun Yang,Wei Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces Entropy-Aware Dense Pruning (EADP), which uses structured compression to improve token pruning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) while maintaining fine-grained visual cues and achieving state-of-the-art (SoTA) results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing token pruning methods for VLMs fail to preserve critical visual information in dense instructions and fine-grained queries due to issues like textual noise and fragmented token selection.

Method: The authors propose EADP, a framework that leverages statistical entropy to filter textual noise and reformulates token selection as a submodular maximization problem with spatial priors to create a comprehensive visual representation.

Result: EADP demonstrates an improved trade-off between accuracy and efficiency in VLMs, preserving critical fine-grained details and achieving SoTA performance on multimodal benchmarks.

Conclusion: Entropy-Aware Dense Pruning (EADP) effectively addresses the challenges of textual noise and feature fragmentation, ensuring high performance for VLMs under strict token budgets.

Abstract: Visual token pruning is a crucial strategy for accelerating VLMs by compressing redundant image patches, yet existing methods often fail to preserve critical cues under dense instructions and fine-grained queries. In this paper, we investigate this failure and identify two underlying bottlenecks: the widespread dispersion of textual noise that corrupts dense cross-modal scoring, and the feature fragmentation inherent to standard token selection. To address these issues, we propose Entropy-Aware Dense Pruning (EADP), a framework that reformulates pruning as a structured compression problem. EADP first leverages statistical entropy to quantify and filter out textual noise, yielding a robust, fine-grained instruction relevance score. Subsequently, instead of naive Top-K selection, EADP casts token selection as a submodular maximization problem with a spatial prior, explicitly ensuring a holistic and non-redundant visual representation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EADP improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off of VLMs, robustly preserving fine-grained visual cues under strict token budgets while achieving SoTA performance on challenging multimodal benchmarks.

</details>


### [269] [GeoMix: Descriptor-Free Visual Localization via Global Context and Multi-Detector Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02486)
*Yejun Zhang,Xinjue Wang,Zihan Wang,Esa Rahtu,Juho Kannala*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoMix introduces a novel framework for descriptor-free visual localization, significantly improving performance by enhancing geometric discriminability and leveraging multi-detector training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the limitations of descriptor-free pipelines in visual localization, including low accuracy due to underutilized geometry cues, lack of global context, and over-reliance on a single keypoint detector.

Method: The proposed GeoMix framework enhances geometric discriminability by enriching local neighbor aggregation, leveraging global context through learnable context nodes, and employing a multi-detector training strategy to optimize representations across various keypoint detectors.

Result: Extensive experiments on several datasets demonstrated that GeoMix surpasses the state-of-the-art descriptor-free methods, achieving significant reductions in rotation and translation errors, while also generalizing effectively to unseen detectors.

Conclusion: GeoMix addresses the key limitations of descriptor-free localization by leveraging advanced geometric matching and training techniques, thereby narrowing the performance gap with descriptor-based pipelines and paving the way for more efficient localization systems.

Abstract: Descriptor-free visual localization eliminates high-dimensional descriptor storage, preserves scene privacy, and simplifies map maintenance, yet its accuracy still lags far behind descriptor-based pipelines. We identify this gap to insufficient geometric discriminability in geometry-only matching. Without visual appearance, current methods underutilize local geometry cues, lack the global context among keypoints, and overfit to a single keypoint detector. We further observe that descriptor-free matching naturally enables multi-detector training, as heterogeneous keypoints can be optimized in a shared geometry-only space without aligning descriptor spaces. Building on these insights, we propose GeoMix, a descriptor-free 2D-3D matching framework that strengthens geometric discriminability at three levels. Locally, directional and distance-aware embeddings enrich neighborhood aggregation with fine-grained spatial structure. Globally, learnable context nodes aggregate and redistribute scene-wide information via cross-attention to resolve ambiguities beyond local receptive fields. At the training level, Mix-Training exploits this detector-agnostic geometry space to learn representations across multiple keypoint detectors. Extensive experiments on MegaDepth, Cambridge Landmarks, 7Scenes, and Aachen Day-Night show that GeoMix sets a new state of the art among descriptor-free methods, reducing 75th-percentile rotation error by 89\% and translation error by up to 90\% over the previous best, while generalizing zero-shot to unseen detectors and narrowing the gap to descriptor-based pipelines. Code is available at $\href{https://github.com/YejunZhang/Geomix}{\text{this links}}$.

</details>


### [270] [Seek to Segment: Active Perception for Panoramic Referring Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02497)
*Song Tang,Shuming Hu,Xincheng Shuai,Henghui Ding,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces Active Panoramic Referring Segmentation (APRS) to address the limitations of static image models in 360-degree environments. They propose PanoSeeker, an advanced memory-augmented agent leveraging Vision-Language Models and spatial memory for efficient exploration and segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing models are limited to static images and fixed perspectives, which are insufficient for Embodied AI tasks in dynamic 360-degree environments.

Method: The proposed model, PanoSeeker, uses EgoSphere spatial memory to integrate observations into a unified representation for efficient exploration. It trains on an annotated dataset followed by reinforcement learning for optimization.

Result: PanoSeeker excels in search efficiency and segmentation accuracy, outperforming adapted state-of-the-art baselines in APRS benchmarks.

Conclusion: PanoSeeker demonstrates significant improvements in handling 360-degree environments, paving the way for more efficient Embodied AI applications.

Abstract: Existing referring segmentation models passively process static images captured from fixed perspectives, limiting their applicability in Embodied AI, where agents must perform active perception in the continuous 360$^\circ$ environments. To bridge this gap, we introduce a novel task: Active Panoramic Referring Segmentation (APRS). In this setting, an agent is required to adjust its viewing direction ($Δθ, Δφ$) to explore the 360$^\circ$ environment, seeking the object specified by a user instruction for segmentation. To tackle this challenging task, we propose PanoSeeker, a memory-augmented agent for efficient APRS. Rather than relying on heuristic scanning, PanoSeeker integrates a Vision-Language Model (VLM) with EgoSphere, an explicit spatial visual memory. By progressively integrating sequential local observations into a unified 360$^\circ$ representation, EgoSphere enables the agent to plan efficient and non-redundant search trajectories. Once the target is found, the agent performs active viewpoint alignment and outputs the segmentation mask. Furthermore, we curate an expert-annotated search trajectory dataset with memory timelines for Supervised Fine-Tuning, followed by Reinforcement Learning post-training to explicitly optimize PanoSeeker's exploration efficiency. Extensive experiments on our newly established APRS benchmark demonstrate that PanoSeeker achieves superior search efficiency and segmentation accuracy, significantly outperforming adapted state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [271] [From SRA to Self-Flow: Data Augmentation or Self-Supervision?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02508)
*Dengyang Jiang,Mengmeng Wang,Harry Yang,Jingdong Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper revisits the mechanism of improvement in recent self-alignment methods like Self-Flow for diffusion transformers. It argues that the improvement stems from data augmentation rather than token interactions across noise levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the underlying mechanism behind the performance gains achieved by dual-time scheduling in diffusion models and explore alternative ways to leverage those findings to boost image generation.

Method: The authors introduce Attention Separation, a method that maintains the dual-timestep input of Self-Flow but explicitly blocks attention between tokens assigned to different noise levels, to test the role of data augmentation versus token interactions.

Result: Attention Separation indicates that the improvements attributed to interactions across noise levels are actually due to data augmentation effects. Further, Attention Separation itself enables augmentation by splitting images into effective training parts, enhancing training data.

Conclusion: The study concludes that data augmentation, rather than token interactions at different noise levels, plays a key role in improving diffusion transformers, leading to a refined training strategy that enhances image generation performance on ImageNet.

Abstract: Representation alignment has become an effective way to accelerate diffusion transformer training and improve generation quality. Recent self-alignment methods, such as SRA and Self-Flow, further remove the dependency on external pretrained encoders by constructing alignment within the diffusion model itself. However, the mechanism behind the improvement from SRA to Self-Flow, dual-time scheduling, remains under-examined: Self-Flow attributes its gain to interactions between tokens at different noise levels, where cleaner tokens help infer noisier ones. In this work, we revisit this explanation and ask whether the gain instead comes from data augmentation along the noise dimension. To disentangle these factors, we introduce Attention Separation, which preserves the same dual-timestep input as Self-Flow while blocking attention between tokens assigned to different noise levels. Surprisingly, removing such interaction does not degrade performance and can even improve it, suggesting that the improvement from SRA to Self-Flow mainly comes from data augmentation. Furthermore,We show that Attention Separation itself provides an augmentation effect by splitting a single image into multiple effective training parts to expand the training data. Based on these observations, we combine self-representation alignment with dual-timestep and attention-separation augmentation, and demonstrate the effectiveness of this design on ImageNet.

</details>


### [272] [PointDiT: Pixel-Space Diffusion for Monocular Geometry Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02515)
*Haofei Xu,Rundi Wu,Philipp Henzler,Nikolai Kalischek,Michael Oechsle,Fabian Manhardt,Marc Pollefeys,Andreas Geiger,Federico Tombari,Michael Niemeyer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a simpler method for single-image 3D reconstruction by using a Diffusion Transformer on raw point maps without complex architecture or latent diffusion spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To simplify single-image 3D reconstruction by removing reliance on complex architectures, latent spaces, and intricate loss functions while achieving superior results.

Method: They propose a Diffusion Transformer leveraging a plain ViT that directly processes raw 3D point map patches and uses image tokens from a pre-trained DINOv3 model. The diffusion model is trained from scratch, bypassing point map tokenizers.

Result: The proposed approach outperforms complex latent-based diffusion models, offering sharper geometry and better robustness, especially for ambiguous regions like transparent objects.

Conclusion: Minimalism in architecture, using pixel-space diffusion on raw 3D point maps, can achieve superior outcomes in 3D reconstruction, proving simpler methods can match or surpass more intricate models.

Abstract: State-of-the-art single-image 3D reconstruction methods often rely on complex hybrid architectures and loss functions, or compress geometry into latent spaces in order to leverage pre-trained latent diffusion models. In this work, we show that such architectural overhead and intricate loss formulations are unnecessary. We introduce a minimalist pixel-space Diffusion Transformer, built on a plain ViT, that operates directly on raw 3D point map patches and is conditioned on image tokens from a pre-trained DINOv3. Unlike existing latent diffusion approaches, we train our diffusion backbone entirely from scratch, eliminating the need for point map tokenizers. Despite its simplicity, our approach surpasses complex latent-based diffusion models while remaining significantly simpler than hybrid alternatives. Notably, it produces sharper geometric structure and is more robust in highly ambiguous regions, such as transparent objects.

</details>


### [273] [Alignment Is All You Need For X-to-4D Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02516)
*Qiaowei Miao,Kehan Li,Yawei Luo,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Align4D is a framework for converting any input modality into video-3D pairs leveraging a novel dataset and three key techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address challenges in arbitrary user-defined modality-to-4D generation, which currently suffer from high costs in dataset creation and limited scalability of existing approaches.

Method: Align4D combines Object Distance Alignment, Motion-Geometry Joint Alignment, and Asynchronous Optimization to align 4D renderings with video and multiview priors, ensure synchronized generation, and decouple training phases for motion and geometry.

Result: Align4D achieves state-of-the-art quality in multimodal X-to-4D generation, validated on the proposed X4D dataset and Consistent4D benchmarks.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates flexibility and scalability by successfully integrating multimodal inputs and achieving coherent, high-quality 4D outputs.

Abstract: Generative diffusion models excel at synthesizing high-quality images, videos, and 3D content under multimodal control. However, arbitrary user-defined modality-to-4D (X-to-4D) generation remains challenging due to the high cost of constructing diverse datasets and the limited scalability of existing methods. This paper presents Align4D, a flexible framework that translates any-modal input into coherent video-3D pairs, using video to guide 4D motion and 3D data to shape 4D geometry. Align4D introduces three key techniques: (1) Object Distance Alignment, which searches Video-Aligned and Multiview-Aligned Object Distances (VAOD/MAOD), respectively, to reconcile 4D renderings with video and the priors of multiview diffusion models; (2) Motion-Geometry Joint Alignment, which constrains known and unknown views through synchronized video and 3D inputs, ensuring consistent 4D generation; and (3) Asynchronous Optimization, which decouples Gaussian attribute and deformation network training to enhance motion and geometry fidelity. We further propose the X4D dataset, which integrates prompt, image, video, and 3D data for benchmarking. Experiments on X4D and Consistent4D demonstrate that Align4D achieves state-of-the-art quality and consistency in X-to-4D generation. Project page: https://miaoqiaowei.github.io/Align4D/.

</details>


### [274] [WorldDirector: Building Controllable World Simulators with Persistent Dynamic Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02517)
*Hanlin Wang,Hao Ouyang,Qiuyu Wang,Wen Wang,Qingyan Bai,Ka Leong Cheng,Yue Yu,Yixuan Li,Yihao Meng,Zichen Liu,Yanhong Zeng,Yujun Shen,Qifeng Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WorldDirector is a framework focused on controlling video world models with persistent object memory and free viewpoint exploration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current world models lack a clear separation between physical dynamics and visual rendering, leading to challenges in maintaining motion and appearance stability without continuous observation.

Method: WorldDirector uses a Large Language Model (LLM) to orchestrate 3D trajectories and camera movements, and employs those as control inputs for video generation.

Result: The system demonstrates the successful synthesis of complex and extended events with precise control and maintains visual identity over time even when objects re-enter the scene after being out of view.

Conclusion: WorldDirector achieves improved controllability and offers advanced capabilities for persistent object memory in video generation, overcoming limitations of existing methods.

Abstract: We present WorldDirector, a highly controllable video world model framework designed for persistent dynamic object memory and unrestricted viewpoint exploration. Unlike existing world models that entangle physical dynamics with pixel rendering and rely on continuous visual observation to sustain motion, our framework explicitly decouples semantic motion orchestration from visual generation. By leveraging an LLM to coordinate 3D trajectories with camera movements and subsequently employing these orchestrated trajectories as control signals for video generation, our approach ensures strict physical logic and appearance stability, successfully preserving the exact visual identities of dynamic entities even when they re-enter the scene after prolonged periods out of view. Experimental results demonstrate that our method supports the synthesis of complex and extended events with unprecedented controllability and persistent dynamic object memory. Project Page: https://worlddirector.github.io/

</details>


<div id='cs.DC'></div>

# cs.DC [[Back]](#toc)

### [275] [HYPIC: Accelerating Hybrid-Attention LLM Serving with Position-Independent Caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01299)
*Yifei Liu,Juntong Wu,Yang Liu,Junhao Hu,Minghao Li,Xiaoxu Chen,Weihang Chen*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces Hypic, a system that combines hybrid-attention models with position-independent caching (PIC), solving challenges in retrieval augmented generation (RAG) and long context agentic LLMs to enhance computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to tackle the computational inefficiencies in large language models (LLMs) during retrieval and generation stages, specifically addressing the inefficiencies caused by the prefill stage when combining hybrid-attention models with position-independent caching (PIC).

Method: The authors developed Hypic by introducing a segment-cumulative transition operator to enable state composition for linear-attention layers and a seam window recomputation technique to handle attention deviation in full-attention layers. They also optimized prefill parallelization to reduce tail latency.

Result: Hypic achieves an average reduction in time-to-first-token (TTFT) by 2.45x and improves peak throughput by up to 2x compared to current systems, while maintaining accuracy within 3.3 points of full recompute.

Conclusion: Hypic effectively resolves previously incompatible techniques—PIC and hybrid-attention models—significantly enhancing computational efficiency in serving large language models without major accuracy compromise.

Abstract: In retrieval augmented generation (RAG) and agentic LLM serving, prompts are assembled from independent segments into long contexts, making the prefill stage dominate the per-request computation cost. To this cost, two directions have emerged in parallel: position-independent caching (PIC) admits KV reuse for non-contiguous segments shared across different requests, while hybrid-attention models reduce computation complexity by replacing most full-attention layers with linear attention. However, they cannot coexist: applying PIC to hybrid-attention models breaks down because per-token KV-cache reuse primitives do not transfer to the per-request recurrent state.
  In this work, we present Hypic, the first serving system for hybrid-attention LLMs with position-independent caching. For linear-attention layers, we identify the segment-cumulative transition operator as the missing algebraic primitive, and cache it alongside each segment's zero-start end-state, enabling near-exact and constant-time state composition of independently cached segments. For the remaining full-attention layers, existing PIC methods also fail as linear layers do not expose the per-token hidden states for selective recomputation. We show that the most significant attention deviation concentrates at segment boundaries, so recomputing only a small seam window at each boundary suffices to restore cross-segment lookback. Finally, Hypic exploits segment-level self-containment to parallelize cache-miss prefill across instances, turning long cold requests -- a major tail-latency contributor under both prefix caching and prior PIC -- into an accelerable workload. Evaluated across four hybrid-attention models and five workloads, Hypic reduces time-to-first-token (TTFT) by 2.45x on average and improves peak throughput by up to 2.0x over existing systems, while staying within 3.3 points of full-recompute accuracy.

</details>


### [276] [SLFS: a Flexible, Low-Cost Distributed File System Using Serverless Designs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01486)
*Cheng Hao,Yang,Paola Alsharabaty,Soufiane Jounaid,Cristina Nita-Rotaru,Ji-Yong Shin*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: SLFS is the first large-scale distributed file system using serverless functions for both data and metadata operations, ensuring high elasticity, cost-effectiveness, and fast response to fluctuating loads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficient resource allocation in traditional distributed file systems caused by peak demand provisioning and fluctuating usage patterns.

Method: SLFS leverages serverless computing to implement file services atop key-value stores, designs a multi-threaded, short-lived server architecture to tackle cold-start issues, and introduces a policy-driven coordinator for cost-performance optimization.

Result: SLFS reduces cold-start delays by 580x compared to traditional serverless designs and delivers better cost-performance compared to other systems (e.g., EFS, Ceph) at significantly reduced cost.

Conclusion: SLFS demonstrates that serverless-based distributed file systems can achieve superior scalability and cost-efficiency, setting a new benchmark for handling dynamic load variations.

Abstract: Large-scale distributed file systems must provision resources for peak demand, yet file access patterns fluctuate significantly, leaving substantial capacity idle during off-peak periods. Existing scaling mechanisms operate at the granularity of entire servers and take minutes to hours, making them unable to track the rapid, fine-grained load variations that file systems commonly experience. Serverless computing, with its millisecond-granularity elasticity and pay-per-use pricing, offers a compelling alternative. We present SLFS, the first distributed file system built with serverless functions for both data and metadata operations. SLFS implements file services on top of key-value stores, keeping function operations simple and short, and introduces a novel multi-threaded, short-lived server design that overcomes the cold-start problem while maintaining low cost. A policy-enforcing coordinator efficiently maps files to function instances, scales the system elastically, and controls function lifetimes to balance performance and cost. SLFS can flexibly run on diverse storage backends -- from cloud-native services like S3 to user-managed key-value stores -- enabling configurable cost-performance trade-offs. Our evaluation shows that SLFS mitigates cold starts by 580$\times$ compared to the base serverless design and outperforms $λ$FS, EFS, and Ceph at up to 63%, 68%, and 63% lower cost, respectively.

</details>


### [277] [OmniPilot: An Uncertainty-Aware LLM Inference Advisor for Heterogeneous GPU Clusters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01579)
*D. Balamurugan,Thomas W. Bush*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces OmniPilot, a launch advisor for configuring large language models (LLMs) on heterogeneous GPU clusters to optimize serving costs, effective throughput, and resource utilization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Choosing optimal configurations for serving LLMs is difficult due to fluctuating performance, operational challenges like hardware failures, and complex trade-offs involving precision, tensor-parallel degrees, and model families.

Method: OmniPilot employs a conformally calibrated quantile cost model paired with an out-of-distribution (OOD) abstention layer. It predicts costs for serving configurations and rates them using an economic utility metric tailored to operator preferences.

Result: In tests with 460 benchmarks across various hardware and precision settings, OmniPilot achieved a 6.2% mean absolute percentage error in throughput prediction, with a log-space $R^2$ of 0.92. Its abstention layer flagged all unsupported scenarios as low-confidence, showcasing effective OOD handling.

Conclusion: OmniPilot effectively assists in selecting optimal configurations for LLM serving, reducing utility regret while maintaining high prediction accuracy. Its OOD generalization capabilities allow iterative improvement to handle more cases over time.

Abstract: Serving large language models (LLMs) on a shared, heterogeneous GPU cluster requires users and operators to select the GPU type, tensor-parallel degree, and precision before committing valuable node-hours. Making these choices is challenging because effective throughput, launch-success rates, and cluster demand and utilization continuously fluctuate. Furthermore, static configuration recipes miss critical interactions: quantization effects depend heavily on the model family, key-value cache pressure creates size-by-precision trade-offs, and failure rates vary by more than twofold across different tensor-parallel degrees. Additionally, cluster resources are frequently constrained by unpredictable hardware failures. To address these challenges, we present \textbf{OmniPilot}, a launch advisor that predicts serving costs for feasible configurations and abstains when requests fall outside its measured support envelope. OmniPilot pairs a conformally calibrated quantile cost model (spanning eight serving targets) with an out-of-distribution (OOD) abstention layer. It ranks configurations using an economic utility metric calibrated to an operator's revealed preferences. In evaluations across 460 benchmark runs on A100, H100, and H200 hardware across four precisions, OmniPilot predicts aggregate throughput with a 6.2\% mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) and a log-space $R^2=0.92$. The advisor achieves 95\% top-1 accuracy with a mean utility regret of just 0.003. When tested on an OOD holdout of unsupported cells, prediction error climbs to 24-46\% and conformal intervals cover 0 of 5 points; however, the abstention layer successfully flags all five as low-confidence. Over time, these OOD scenarios will be integrated into the training dataset to continuously expand the advisor's support envelope.

</details>


### [278] [Arachne: Orchestrating Cascades for Efficient Text-to-Video Model Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01701)
*Peng Yu,Yuankai Fan,Yang Qiu,Tian Li,Bihuan Chen,Yin Chen,Qizhen Weng*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: Arachne is a new framework that optimizes Text-to-Video (T2V) model training by addressing workload imbalances and hardware inefficiencies in current methods, achieving up to 65% faster iteration times.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses inefficiencies in existing training methods for Text-to-Video (T2V) models, specifically workload imbalance and hardware under-utilization caused by static parallelism schemes and bucketing strategies.

Method: Arachne introduces a fine-grained computational unit called 'cascades' and employs coordinated spatial and temporal optimization for distributed execution and synchronization across training clusters.

Result: Arachne improves iteration time by up to 65% compared to leading frameworks and demonstrates better scalability as training scale increases.

Conclusion: Arachne is an effective solution for scaling T2V model training, optimizing resource utilization and addressing the limitations of current methods, making it highly efficient for large-scale applications.

Abstract: The rising demand for AI-generated videos is fueled by advances in large-scale Text-to-Video (T2V) models, trained on extensive datasets of video clips spanning diverse resolutions and durations. To address this data heterogeneity, current training methods often use a bucketing strategy that groups samples into discrete buckets for efficiency. However, this approach struggles to scale with compute and data volumes under static parallelism schemes, such as data and sequence parallelism, leading to significant workload imbalances and hardware under-utilization.
  In this paper, we present Arachne, a novel training framework for efficient T2V model training at scale. Arachne decomposes the training process into fine-grained computational units, called \textit{cascades}, orchestrating their distributed execution and synchronization across the cluster through coordinated spatial and temporal optimization. Our comprehensive evaluation demonstrates that Arachne reduces iteration time by up to 65\% over leading frameworks, exhibiting a positive scaling trend where its performance advantages amplify as training scale grows.

</details>


### [279] [Exploiting Task-Based Parallelism for the Red-Black Gauss-Seidel Method on 2D Grids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01735)
*Shiting Long,Gustavo Ramirez-Hidalgo,Andreas Frommer,Dirk Pleiter*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: This paper investigates task-based programming models for implementing the red-black Gauss-Seidel method, aiming to improve efficiency and resilience compared to conventional divide-and-conquer strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiencies in conventional parallelization strategies for multi-color Gauss-Seidel due to global synchronization and load imbalance.

Method: Compare task-based programming models and divide-and-conquer parallel implementations of the red-black Gauss-Seidel method using 2D Poisson equation benchmarks.

Result: Task-based implementations demonstrated comparable performance to divide-and-conquer methods while offering increased resilience to hardware asynchronicity.

Conclusion: Task-based programming models are effective for fine-grained parallelism, improving execution efficiency and reliability in iterative solution methods.

Abstract: Gauss-Seidel is a well-established iterative method for the solution of linear systems, and multicoloring has been widely used to increase parallelism in iterative solution techniques. Implementing multi-color Gauss-Seidel with conventional divide-and-conquer parallelization strategies, however, may be inefficient due to global synchronization requirements and load imbalances. Task-based programming models can mitigate these issues by enabling fine-grained parallelism, removing global barriers and allowing updates of different colors to partially overlap in time. In this work, we implement the red-black Gauss-Seidel method using two task-based programming models and compare them with a classical divide-and-conquer parallel implementation to evaluate the impact of fine-grained parallelism on execution efficiency. The red-black scheme serves as a representative example, as task-based approaches naturally extend to more general multi-color schemes arising from unstructured grids and wider stencils. Using the solve of the 2D Poisson equation as benchmark, our results show that task-based implementations can achieve performance comparable to conventional divide-and-conquer parallelization while providing greater resilience to hardware-level asynchronicity.

</details>


### [280] [HCMS: Head-Chunked Multi-Stream Pipeline for Communication-Computation Overlap in Long-Sequence Parallel Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01817)
*Chao Yuan,Pan Li,Yingnan Sun,Jing Liu*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces HCMS, a method that improves computation speed in all-to-all sequence parallelism methods for medium-long sequences by exploiting multi-head attention using multiple CUDA streams, achieving significant speedups over existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: All-to-all based sequence parallelism methods suffer from resource underutilization for medium-long sequences due to serially executed communication and computation. The research aims to address this inefficiency and improve hardware resource utilization.

Method: HCMS achieves better efficiency by partitioning attention heads into chunks and overlapping computation and communication using dual CUDA streams. It works with existing optimizations, requires no kernel modifications, and supports uneven partitioning.

Result: Experiments show that HCMS offers 10%-17.5% speedup over Ulysses and 5%-14.5% over Ring Attention for certain sequence lengths, with 6.8% end-to-end acceleration on a model. Theoretical models indicate benefits are linked to the communication ratio.

Conclusion: HCMS provides a robust and resource-efficient solution for handling medium-long sequence parallelism with significant performance improvements, especially if the communication ratio is high (>20%).

Abstract: All-to-all based sequence parallelism methods execute communication and computation strictly in serial when processing medium-long sequences, resulting in hardware resource underutilization. This paper proposes Head-Chunked Multi-Stream Pipeline (HCMS), which exploits the computational independence of multi-head attention by partitioning attention heads into multiple chunks and achieving fine-grained communication-computation overlap through dual CUDA streams. HCMS is orthogonally compatible with existing optimizations such as FlashAttention and SDPA, requires no modification to underlying kernels, supports uneven partitioning while maintaining numerical equivalence. Experiments validate the effectiveness across four GPU platforms at 2-8 GPU scales: for typical video generation sequence lengths of 31K-56K tokens, HCMS achieves 10\%-17.5\% speedup over the Ulysses baseline and 5\%-14.5\% speedup over Ring Attention; end-to-end acceleration of 6.8\% is achieved on the Wan2.2 model. Theoretical analysis shows that HCMS benefits are positively correlated with communication ratio $ρ$, and its use is recommended when $ρ>20\%$.

</details>


### [281] [Lynx: Progressive Speculative Quantization for accelerating KV Transfer in Long-Context Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01831)
*Wenchen Han,Gingfung Matthew Yeung,Marco Barletta,William Toner,Amory Hoste,Adam Barker*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces Lynx, a system for faster long-context inference in LLM serving by splitting high-precision Key-Value (KV) caches into streams, allowing progressive decoding during data transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address latency and accuracy bottlenecks in long-context inference workloads of LLMs, where transferring large KV caches delays the decoding process despite recent quantization techniques.

Method: The authors propose Lynx, which partitions KV caches into two streams: a high-priority Anchor stream with significant bits, and a low-priority Residual stream with less impactful bits. Decoding starts with the Anchor stream, running speculatively, and is verified when the Residual stream completes transfer.

Result: Lynx achieves comparable latency to aggressive 4-bit KV quantization while maintaining high-precision inference accuracy (BF16), improving TTFT over 8-bit KV by up to 1.43× and accuracy by up to 5.1%.

Conclusion: Lynx provides a way to reduce network-exposed latency during LLM serving, achieving both high accuracy and reduced TTFT, overcoming limitations of prior KV quantization schemes.

Abstract: Long-context inference is increasingly common in large language model (LLM) serving, driven by retrieval-augmented generation and agentic systems. In disaggregated inference, these workloads require transferring large Key-Value (KV) caches across the network, where decoding cannot begin until the transfer completes. Recent KV quantization techniques reduce data volume and alleviate this bottleneck, but existing schemes fail to achieve both low network-exposed latency and high inference accuracy.
  We challenge the assumption that the KV cache is an indivisible unit that must be fully received before use. We leverage the observation that different bits in the KV cache contribute unequally to attention computation and inference precision: the most significant bits capture the coarse structure of attention and the least significant bits refine precision. This property enables partial use of the KV cache during decoding. We present Lynx, a system that enables progressive, split-stream KV transfer by partitioning the KV cache into a high-priority Anchor stream carrying the most significant bits and a low-priority Residual stream carrying remaining precision. Decoding begins upon receipt of the Anchor stream and proceeds speculatively while the Residual stream is transferred concurrently, followed by verification that ensures equivalence to higher-precision decoding.
  Across multiple models and serving workloads, Lynx achieves Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) comparable to aggressive 4-bit KV quantization, while matching the accuracy of high-precision (BF16) inference, improving TTFT over standard 8-bit KV quantization by up to $1.43\times$ and improving accuracy over state-of-the-art by up to $5.1\%$.

</details>


### [282] [Mixture-of-Parallelisms: Towards Memory-Efficient Training Stack for Mixture-of-Experts Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01844)
*Xuan-Phi Nguyen,Shrey Pandit,Yiran Zhao,Semih Yavuz,Silvio Savarese,Shafiq Joty*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper presents a memory-efficient approach for training massive Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, achieving improved throughput and scalability beyond prior benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling up Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models to trillion-parameter sizes presents significant computational and memory challenges, requiring novel techniques to maximize hardware efficiency and enable large-scale training.

Method: The study introduces a specialized training paradigm combining existing and novel parallelism techniques optimized across the MoE training pipeline. A novel optimizer step is also proposed for achieving high throughput and memory efficiency.

Result: The approach achieves 4.7x-8.2x higher throughput per GPU compared to a strong FSDP2 baseline and supports context lengths up to 1M tokens where other techniques fail due to memory limits.

Conclusion: This paradigm allows researchers to train trillion-parameter models with high efficiency on limited GPU resources, marking a significant advance in large-scale AI training methods.

Abstract: This paper showcases a memory-efficient training stack for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models. It is a training paradigm that combines and specializes various existing and novel parallelism techniques at different layers and stages of the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model training pipeline. It leverages these techniques to achieve maximal efficiency given the physical constraints of CPU, CPU memory, GPU HBM memory, and the CPU-GPU, GPU-GPU, and node-node communication bandwidth of the GPU cluster. It also contains a novel strategy for the optimizer step to achieve high throughput and memory efficiency, enabling practitioners to conduct lossless pre-training/fine-tuning of trillion-parameter scale models, at a million context length, with just under 12 8x H200 GPU nodes, with state-of-the-art throughput and memory efficiency. In our experiments, MoP delivers 4.7x--8.2x higher per-GPU throughput than a strongly-tuned FSDP2 baseline (with the gap widening at larger scale) and sustains training at context lengths up to 1M tokens, where the baseline runs out of memory beyond 64--128K.

</details>


### [283] [Towards Load-Aware Prefill Deflection for Disaggregated LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02043)
*Shrikara Arun,Anjaly Parayil,Srikant Bharadwaj,Renee St. Amant,Victor Rühle*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new scheduler for disaggregated LLM serving to reduce latency in bursty workloads by allowing decode GPUs to assist with prefill tasks, eliminating delays caused by inter-node communication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address inefficiencies in disaggregated LLM serving where prefill nodes become a bottleneck and decode nodes are underutilized, leading to high latency in bursty workloads.

Method: They propose a proactive scheduler that allows decode nodes to handle prefill tasks in chunks while ensuring decode tasks meet their latency requirements, avoiding inter-node KV-cache transfer delays.

Result: The proposed method reduces the 95th percentile Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) by up to 81% and improves Service Level Objective (SLO) attainment by up to 79% compared to existing schedulers.

Conclusion: This proactive scheduler significantly improves the performance of disaggregated LLM serving by dynamically distributing prefill tasks to decode nodes, reducing latency, and increasing efficiency at minimal routing overhead.

Abstract: Disaggregated LLM serving runs prefill and decode on separate GPU pools to keep the two phases from interfering. In practice, this creates a new asymmetry: under bursty, heavy-tailed workloads prefill nodes saturate while decode nodes have compute underutilized, and on a production-style A100 cluster with 2 prefill and 2 decode nodes (2P2D), we find that prefill execution accounts for only 2-23% of P95 Time-to-First-Token (TTFT). Queuing and inter-node GPU-GPU KV-cache transfer account for the rest. We present a proactive prefill-deflecting scheduler that lets decode nodes serve prefill phase of requests as chunked-prefill steps interleaved with their in-flight decode batches. For each queued request, we estimate the TTFT it would see on the prefill node, and on every decode node, search for the largest chunk schedule that keeps in-flight decodes within their Time-Between-Tokens (TBT) SLO and deflect when the decode path helps tail latency. Because the prefill phase of deflected requests runs in place on the decode node, the inter-node KV transfer is eliminated. Implemented on vLLM and evaluated on production-style traces with DeepSeek-V2-Lite, our approach reduces P95 TTFT by upto 81% and raises SLO attainment by upto 79% over state-of-the-art disaggregated schedulers, at sub-millisecond per-request routing cost.

</details>


### [284] [Cadence: Extreme Pipelining with Multiple Concurrent Proposers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02275)
*Fatima Elsheimy,Mohammad Mussadiq Jalalzai,Tobias Klenze,Jovan Komatovic,Mike Setrin,Victor Shoup,Kushal Babel,Lioba Heimbach,Jason Milionis*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: Cadence is a multi-proposer Byzantine fault-tolerant consensus protocol with extreme pipelining to achieve fast transaction finalizations and optimal latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to overcome limitations of single-leader consensus such as monopolies on transaction inclusion and inefficient block intervals dependent on network latency.

Method: Cadence achieves its objectives using extreme pipelining, multiple concurrent proposers (MCP), and a novel framework that builds multi-shot protocols from one-shot consensus. It introduces custom protocols called Chorus for fast block finalization and Conductor for slot orchestration.

Result: Cadence achieves short-term censorship resistance, hiding, and proves safety, liveness, and performance guarantees under partial synchrony and optimal resilience in simulations.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that Cadence is the first MCP protocol achieving censorship resistance and hiding at the fast-path latency of single-leader consensus, with significant performance and security improvements.

Abstract: We present Cadence, a Byzantine fault-tolerant multi-proposer consensus protocol with arbitrarily low block intervals, optimal resilience, and optimal fast-path latency. Cadence divides time into equally spaced slots, one block per slot, each finalized in its own consensus instance. Blocks do not build directly on their predecessor, so instances run independently and none waits for an earlier block to finish or propagate; we call this extreme pipelining, decoupling the block interval from network latency. Cadence also removes the single-leader monopoly over transaction inclusion and ordering: under multiple concurrent proposers (MCP), several validators propose for each block, and it guarantees that, under synchrony, a transaction a correct proposer includes cannot be censored or deferred (short-term censorship resistance), and that no proposer can craft its proposal in reaction to the others' (hiding). To realize extreme pipelining, we introduce a general framework that turns any one-shot consensus meeting our slot-consensus specification into a multi-shot protocol. We instantiate it for MCP with two protocols of our own: Chorus, a slot consensus whose fast path finalizes a block in an optimal three rounds, with speculative finality one round earlier, and Conductor, an orchestrator that opens slots at an even cadence, more slowly under asynchrony to keep open slots bounded. To our knowledge, Cadence is the first MCP protocol to provide short-term censorship resistance and hiding at the fast-path latency of single-leader consensus. We prove safety, liveness, censorship resistance, and hiding under partial synchrony with optimal resilience (n = 3f+1). In simulation over Monad's 200 validators with five proposers per slot, finalization averages 219 ms (167 ms to speculative finality); at a 100 ms block interval a transaction waits on average 50 ms to enter a proposal.

</details>


### [285] [Securing People and their Machines Against Major Faults](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02304)
*Ohad Eitan,Idit Keidar,Ehud Shapiro*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper addresses security in grassroots platforms, proposing a peer-based recovery solution involving social graphs, identity custodians, and state custodians for recovering from private key or device loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve the issue of securing grassroots platforms against major faults, such as loss of private keys or smartphones, without relying on central resources for recovery.

Method: The solution uses a grassroots social graph, where agents maintain friendships, designate identity custodians, and enlist state custodians for secure transactions and recovery in case of identity loss or device replacement.

Result: The proposed system enables identity recovery through social graph interactions with identity custodians and restores lost devices or keys securely. It achieves fault recoverability, proved mathematically.

Conclusion: The research provides a secure recovery mechanism for grassroots platforms, showing practical feasibility via implementations that align with the proven specifications.

Abstract: We consider grassroots platforms -- distributed systems of agents consisting of people identified by self-chosen public keys and their machines (smartphones) -- and wish to make them secure against \emph{major faults}: the loss of their private keys and/or their smartphones. As grassroots platforms have no global resource to rely on for recovery, our peer-based solution is based on: (\ia) \emph{a grassroots social graph} in which agents establish and maintain friendships; (\ib) \emph{identity custodians}, designated by each person, and (\ic) \emph{state custodians}, which are grassroots platform-specific. Upon a person experiencing identity loss, and given a willing supermajority of the identity custodians of the person, the friends of the person replace the old public key with the new one across the graph and restore friendships, where all friends serve as state custodians for the social graph. Choosing a new keypair, obtaining a new smartphone, and convincing identity custodians to will a change of key all happen ``off-chain''. Recovery from machine loss without loss of key (e.g. smartphone run over by truck, or its memory wiped) is simpler, requiring only the help of state custodians.
  We specify the social graph and its secure version as guarded multiagent atomic transactions, and implement the secure social graph via communicating volitional agents, an eventually synchronous message-passing model one step closer to implementation. We prove the implementation maps runs with recoverable faults to correct runs of the specification.
  We follow a similar path for grassroots coins and bonds, showing a common core as well as the platform-specific aspects of state recovery: a currency's single-writer log is recovered exactly, the recovered sovereign resuming without double-spending.

</details>


### [286] [Elasticity in Parallel Sparse Triangular Solve](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02324)
*Raphael S. Steiner,Christos K. Matzoros,Pál András Papp,Toni Böhnlein,A. N. Yzelman*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: This paper introduces stale synchronous parallel execution for sparse triangular linear system solving, achieving notable speed-ups with their ElasticDivide scheduler over existing methods on both ARM and x86 machines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve execution efficiency in parallel sparse triangular linear system solving by reducing synchronization overhead.

Method: They propose a stale synchronous parallel execution model and develop a general directed-acyclic-graph scheduler called ElasticDivide.

Result: ElasticDivide achieves a geometric-mean speed-up of 7-30% over GrowLocal on an ARM machine and 19-60% over SpMP on an x86 machine with 48 cores.

Conclusion: ElasticDivide demonstrates significant performance gains, showcasing the potential of stale synchronous parallel scheduling in reducing synchronization delays in parallel computing.

Abstract: We introduce stale synchronous parallel as a mode of execution in parallel sparse triangular linear system solve and present a general directed-acyclic-graph scheduler capable of producing such schedules. Stale-synchronous-parallel schedules allow the overlap of synchronisation and compute which results in a geometric-mean speed-up of $7$-$30\%$ of our scheduler, ElasticDivide, over state-of-the-art synchronous scheduler GrowLocal on an ARM machine using 48 cores. On an x86 machine using 48 cores, we report geometric-mean speed-ups of $19$-$60\%$ over SpMP.

</details>


### [287] [WattGPU: Predicting Inference Power and Latency on Unseen GPUs and LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02391)
*Mauricio Fadel Argerich,Jonathan Fürst,Marta Patiño-Martínez*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: This paper introduces WattGPU, a model to predict GPU power efficiency and latency for LLM inference without profiling data, achieving higher accuracy and generalizability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data center energy consumption is increasingly driven by LLM inference, and matching LLMs to the most energy-efficient GPUs is difficult without extensive profiling.

Method: The authors propose predictive models for GPU power draw and latency, using publicly available LLM metadata and GPU specifications, validated through leave-one-out cross-validation on unseen GPUs and LLMs.

Result: WattGPU shows high accuracy, achieving ≤3.4% error for offline and ≤13.5% for server power predictions on unseen GPUs, with significant improvements over traditional baselines.

Conclusion: WattGPU provides an effective, generalizable solution for predicting GPU efficiency for LLM inference, reducing the need for costly profiling and improving deployment optimization.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) inference workloads are a rapidly growing contributor to data center energy consumption. Optimizing these deployments requires matching specific LLMs to the most efficient GPUs, but operators currently lack the tools to do so without exhaustively profiling each combination. While some predictive models exist, they still require profiling data and struggle to generalize to hardware unseen during training. To address this, we introduce \textit{WattGPU}, featuring two predictive models for mean GPU power draw and Inter-Token Latency (ITL). Our approach leverages only publicly available LLM metadata and GPU specifications, eliminating the need for hardware access or profiling while enabling generalization to unseen NVIDIA server-grade GPUs and LLMs. We evaluate our models using rigorous leave-one-GPU-out and leave-one-LLM-out cross-validation on a dataset of 42 open-source LLMs (0.1B--27B parameters) and 8 GPUs under both offline and server scenarios. The mean power draw model achieves a median absolute percentage error of $\leq3.4\%$ for offline and $\leq13.5\%$ for server scenarios on unseen GPUs, while the latency model achieves $\leq8.5\%$ in server mode, both maintaining strong GPU ranking correlations for server scenarios (Kendall $τ\geq0.76$). Compared to standard physically grounded baselines -- Load-Scaled Thermal Design Power (TDP) for power draw and roofline for latency -- our models reduce median absolute percentage error by approximately 4$\times$ on unseen LLM-GPU combinations for server scenarios or approximately 2$\times$ for completely unseen GPUs. WattGPU's data and code are publicly available at https://github.com/maufadel/wattgpu.

</details>


### [288] [FlintKV: A Fast Durable Storage Engine for Modern Databases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02401)
*Sergey Egorov,Gregory Chockler,Brijesh Dongol,Dan O'Keeffe,Sadegh Keshavarzi*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: FlintKV is an NVM-optimized storage engine providing advanced features (e.g., atomic batch operations and consistent iterations) essential for modern database applications, delivering up to 75% better throughput than prior work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the shortcomings of earlier NVM key-value stores, which prioritize high throughput but lack essential modern database features like point-in-time snapshots, atomic operations, and consistency guarantees.

Method: The authors developed FlintKV, a skiplist-based storage engine optimized for non-volatile memory. They introduced a novel flat-combining concurrency control algorithm with multi-versioning and persistence techniques to achieve high scalability and performance.

Result: FlintKV demonstrated a substantial improvement, achieving up to 75% higher end-to-end throughput compared to previous solutions.

Conclusion: FlintKV provides a robust solution for NVM-optimized storage with full API support for production-level key-value stores, effectively balancing high throughput and rich feature support for database needs.

Abstract: Byte-addressable non-volatile memory (NVM) offers an opportunity to rethink storage engine architectures. While recent NVM key-value stores achieve high throughput for ingestion and point lookups, they omit or under-specify the support for the richer interface guarantees required by modern databases. Production key-value engines (e.g., RocksDB) provide point-in-time snapshots, consistent iterators, and atomic batches-features essential for implementing transactions and concurrency control. We present FlintKV, an NVM-optimized skiplist-based storage engine that natively supports the full API of production key-value stores. FlintKV supports both atomic batch writes and snapshot-consistent iteration efficiently while guaranteeing durable linearizability. FlintKV can be deployed standalone or its durable skiplist can be integrated into existing NVM stores to enhance their capabilities. Central to FlintKV is a novel flat-combining based concurrency control algorithm that leverages multi-versioning and carefully co-designed persistence mechanisms to ensure high performance and scalability. Our empirical evaluation shows that FlintKV can achieve up to a 75% improvement in end-to-end throughput over prior work.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [289] [Multilayer Q-Matrix-Embedded Neural Network for Cognitive Diagnosis (M-QCDNet): Structure-Aware Deep Learning Architecture for Psychometric Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01278)
*Yiyao Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces M-QCDNet, a neural network for cognitive diagnosis that maintains interpretability by integrating structural elements from traditional cognitive diagnostic models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for integrating interpretability, structural validity, and predictive performance in cognitive diagnostic models using neural networks.

Method: The authors designed M-QCDNet, embedding a Q-matrix as structural prior to constrain item-skill relationships, and introduced a loss function with an L2 penalty for alignment with cognitive theories. Additionally, they developed interpretability metrics for evaluation.

Result: M-QCDNet achieved interpretable and structurally aligned latent skill representations while balancing predictive performance, supporting its utility in educational settings.

Conclusion: M-QCDNet bridges the gap between interpretability and advanced neural capabilities, offering an actionable and transparent framework for cognitive diagnostics applicable to educational practices.

Abstract: The research proposes a multilayer Q-matrix-embedded neural network for cognitive diagnosis (M-QCDNet), which integrates the structural interpretability of cognitive diagnostic models (CDMs) with the deep learning neural network (NN). M-QCDNet structures the item-skill relationship using the Q-matrix as a structural prior, ensuring latent mastery profiles remain interpretable and consistent with cognitive theory, followed by the proposed loss function with an L2 penalty to penalize skills not aligned with the Q-matrix and to balance predictive performance and structural alignment. Corresponding evaluation matrices, the interpretable alignment-based metrics that quantify the degree to which predicted skill activations correspond to item-level skills, were further developed. M-QCDNet offers practical benefits for classroom practice, enabling early detection of learning difficulties and supporting mastery-based interventions. By embedding diagnostic validity into model design, M-QCDNet bridges psychometric transparency and neural flexibility, advancing interpretable, fair, and actionable AI for cognitive diagnostics.

</details>


### [290] [I\textsuperscript{2}RiMA: Spectral Riemannian Representation with Temporal Attention for Mental Stress Detection based on EEG Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01279)
*Cheng He,Kunyu Peng,Shangen Han,Jinming Ma,Jinhong Ding,Likun Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel Riemannian Manifold Attention Network (method{}) for EEG-based stress detection, achieving high accuracy and efficiency by leveraging frequency-specific spatial covariance and temporal dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
