Agreement with human grading is measured at both the question and exam-total levels using mean absolute error, root mean squared error, normalized root mean squared error, Pearson correlation, and exact agreement. The results show that liberal partial-credit prompting reduces average question-level error for every evaluated model family. ChatGPT 5.5 Thinking (LIBERAL) has the lowest average question-level MAE (1.87) and RMSE (2.53), while Gemini 3.1 Pro Extended (LIBERAL) has the lowest total-score MAE (8.00) and RMSE (10.66). However, the strongest total-score Pearson correlation occurs under Gemini 3.1 Pro Extended (BASELINE) at 0.58, showing that point calibration and rank preservation remain distinct goals. We also report practical usability observations.

</details>


### [494] [A Practice Auditing Framework for Large Language Model Use: Collective Empiricism, Pseudo-Rational Cognition, and Governance of AI-Generated Content](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01248)
*Yang Zhao,Yingshuo Li,Zeyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework to audit risks associated with using Large Language Models (LLMs) for tasks like knowledge acquisition and automation, emphasizing the importance of validating AI outputs and mitigating cognitive risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users may mistake structured AI-generated contents for their own rational understanding, leading to risks like memory pollution and statistical misjudgment, necessitating guidelines for safe AI interaction and governance.

Method: The paper proposes a practice auditing framework consisting of processes like requirement definition, evidence-source auditing, validation, reverse questioning, logging, and rollback mechanisms.

Result: The framework emphasizes making LLM outputs verifiable, reproducible, and intervenable, reducing cognitive and practical risks while retaining AI productivity.

Conclusion: A structured auditing process can enable responsible governance of LLM outputs and mitigate risks in human-AI interaction and AI-generated content usage.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used for knowledge acquisition, code generation, academic writing, and agent-based automation. In these settings, users may obtain highly structured answers, plans, and judgments without sufficient domain practice. This paper proposes a practice auditing framework for LLM use and AI-generated content governance. It introduces collective empiricism to describe how LLMs compress and reorganize large-scale human experience into outputs that appear empirical and rational, and pseudo-rational cognition to describe how users may mistake AI-generated structured expression for their own rational understanding. The paper analyzes AI subjectivity illusion, subjectivity structures in input materials, template loops in AI-AI conversations, statistical misjudgment in AIGC detection, and memory pollution when generated content enters future contexts, long-term memory, retrieval spaces, or agent skill systems. To reduce these risks, the paper proposes an auditing process based on requirement definition, problem-boundary identification, evidence-source auditing, practical validation, reverse questioning, logging, version management, rollback, and renewed cognition. The framework does not reject AI productivity; it argues that LLM outputs should be returned to verifiable, reproducible, and intervenable processes of practice. The paper provides a conceptual and auditable framework for cognitive risks in LLM interaction, AI-generated content governance, long-term memory systems, and human-AI interaction.

</details>


### [495] [Collaborative Disagreement Resolution for Scalable Oversight](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01251)
*Yuyang Jiang,Chacha Chen,Teng Wu,Liwen Sun,Han Liu,Shi Feng,Chenhao Tan*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: This paper introduces 'Disagreement Resolution,' a collaborative truth-seeking paradigm as an alternative to adversarial AI debates for scalable oversight, demonstrating enhanced accuracy in identifying the truth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the misalignment between the persuasiveness-focused incentives in AI debates and epistemic honesty, proposing a paradigm that emphasizes collaborative truth-seeking.

Method: The authors created an automated pipeline inspired by human mediation techniques, designed for models to identify disagreements, evaluate evidence, and work collaboratively toward consensus or pinpoint the core disagreement.

Result: The Disagreement Resolution method achieved a judging accuracy of 62.1%, significantly outperforming the 49.2% accuracy of standard debate approaches.

Conclusion: The study suggests a shift from adversarial AI debates to collaborative truth-seeking frameworks, showcasing a more effective and honest approach for scalable oversight.

Abstract: Debate, where AI agents argue opposing positions, has emerged as a key approach to scalable oversight. However, debate faces a fundamental tension: models are incentivized to be persuasive to the judge, which may not always align with epistemic honesty. In this work, we propose an alternative paradigm: disagreement resolution, which reframes the interaction mechanism from adversarial debate to collaborative truth seeking. Drawing on principles from human mediation and conflict resolution, where mediators facilitate dialogue to help disputing parties reach consensus rather than adjudicating between them, we design an automated pipeline that adapts these strategies to AI oversight. Unlike standard debate where models argue for fixed positions, our pipeline directs models to collaboratively identify points of disagreement, examine the evidence for conflicting claims, and converge toward consensus or isolate the specific ''crux'' of their disagreement. We find that Disagreement Resolution consistently helps non-expert models identify the truth, achieving 62.1% judging accuracy compared to 49.2% for standard debate. Our results provide encouraging empirical evidence for rethinking the scalable oversight protocol from adversarial persuasion to collaborative truth-seeking.

</details>


### [496] [How Indian Dermatologists are Utilizing Artificial Intelligence for Clinical Practice and Workflow Management: A Nationwide Survey with a Special Focus on atopic dermatitis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01252)
*Dipayan Sengupta,Saumya Panda,Sandipan Dhar,Dipankar De,Deepika Pandhi,Narayanan B*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: A survey of Indian dermatologists revealed a gap between the clinical challenges in chronic dermatology disease workflows like atopic dermatitis (AD) and the current AI applications, which are mainly non-specialized and administrative.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The aim was to understand the practical challenges faced by dermatologists, specifically in managing chronic conditions like AD, and to evaluate the extent and manner of AI integration in their workflows.

Method: A nationwide cross-sectional survey among 377 practicing Indian dermatologists was conducted. Clinical challenges, barriers in AD workflow, current AI applications, and ethical concerns were analyzed using statistical and regression techniques.

Result: Patient adherence and treatment planning in difficult cases were more challenging than diagnostic uncertainty. Severity scoring in AD had the lowest satisfaction. Around 50% used AI mainly for cognitive and administrative purposes. Barriers to adoption differed by professional experience. AI users expressed more concerns about patient self-misdiagnosis and anxiety.

Conclusion: AI applications in dermatology are currently misaligned with clinical needs; AI tools tailored to clinician-supervised workflow support for chronic disease management would be more impactful.

Abstract: Background: Dermatology AI has mainly focused on image-based diagnosis, while chronic disease workflows have received less attention. We surveyed Indian dermatologists to map routine clinical challenges, with a focus on atopic dermatitis (AD), and assess current AI use.
  Methods: A nationwide cross-sectional survey commissioned by the Society for Eczema Studies included 377 practicing Indian dermatologists. The survey assessed clinical challenges, AD workflow barriers, AI use, adoption barriers, and ethical concerns. Analyses used descriptive statistics, chi-square tests, false discovery rate correction, and multivariable logistic regression.
  Results: Patient adherence (61.3%) and treatment planning in difficult or refractory cases (57.0%) were reported more often than diagnostic uncertainty (48.0%). In AD care, severity scoring was reported as a challenge by 47.7% and had the lowest satisfaction among measured workflow areas. Current AI use was reported by 49.9%, most often involving general large language models for literature synthesis, documentation, and academic tasks rather than specialized image analysis. Barriers differed by experience: dermatologists with more than 20 years of practice more often cited lack of training, while those with 5 years or less more often cited lack of clinical utility after trying AI tools. AI users were more likely than non-users to report concern about patient self-misdiagnosis and anxiety, which remained significant after adjustment for experience and academic affiliation.
  Conclusion: Respondents reported using general-purpose AI mainly for cognitive and administrative tasks, while their clinical needs centered on chronic disease management and AD workflow support. Clinician-supervised workflow tools may be more useful than standalone diagnostic applications.

</details>


### [497] [Beyond Detection: Redesigning Assessment and Governande of Generative AI at the Universidad Politécnica de Madrid (UPM)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01255)
*Jessica Díaz,Sonia Linio,Fernando Pescador,Daniel Martin-Fabiani*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper critiques universities' defensive reactions to generative AI, advocating instead for its strategic and educational integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the prevailing defensive approach by universities to generative AI and advocate for its potential as a tool for pedagogical innovation.

Method: Analyzing the limitations of AI detection mechanisms and proposing a comprehensive strategic framework implemented at Universidad Politécnica de Madrid (UPM).

Result: The paper provides a framework focusing on authentic assessments, AI-literacy programs, and clear guidelines for integrating AI into education.

Conclusion: Universities should embrace generative AI by rethinking assessment, fostering interdisciplinary learning, and developing student autonomy, while addressing broader organizational and legal challenges.

Abstract: Universities have responded to generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) in noticeably different ways, both internationally and within Spain. So far, the dominant reaction has been defensive, this is, most institutions frame the debate around AI detection, plagiarism, academic integrity and a presumed drop in student effort, prioritizing basic training for academic staff over students. Other group of pioneering universities is doing the opposite, pursuing deeper adoption, and assuming that any policy built on prevention or sanction will not hold. This paper sides with that second view. Obsessing about detection is a dead end, since generated text is increasingly hard to distinguish from human writing, and detectors still misfire too often to be trusted. What universities need instead is a coordinated effort to set clear, course-by-course rules for GenAI use, redesign assessment toward authentic and interdisciplinary assessment that fosters critical thinking and learner autonomy, and build a serious AI-literacy programme that treats students as critical co-creators rather than passive users. The challenge, though, is not only pedagogical. Adoption at university scale also raises organisational, technical, operational, legal and economic questions that have to be solved together. In this context, the Universidad Politécnica de Madrid (UPM) is developing a strategic and sustainable AI policy and adoption framework structured around six dimensions, in which AI functions as an enabler of student autonomy and pedagogical innovation rather than as a threat to be policed.

</details>


### [498] [AI Assistance for Human Review of Default Judgments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01256)
*Theodora Worledge,Othman Bensouda Koraichi,Daniel Bernal,Aviv Caspi,Tatsunori Hashimoto,Carlos Guestrin,David Freeman Engstrom*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: U.S. courts face challenges reviewing default judgments, with significant error rates. An AI tool, Default Assistant, improves accuracy by 6% and speed by 25.9% in simulated reviews.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: U.S. courts are overwhelmed by default judgment reviews, prone to errors, and lack adequate resources. Enhancing accuracy and efficiency is essential.

Method: Researchers designed and tested Default Assistant using large language models (LLMs) for cited recommendations, enabling users to cross-verify recommendations with original filings. A controlled study was conducted with 66 law students.

Result: The Default Assistant improved review accuracy by 6.0%, sped up review time by 25.9%, and achieved significant performance gains in statutory requirements relying on document searches.

Conclusion: AI tools like Default Assistant, with citation capabilities, can aid resource-constrained courts in performing more accurate and timely default judgment reviews.

Abstract: Overwhelmed courts in the United States review millions of default judgments each year. Unfortunately, such manual reviews are time-consuming and prone to error. In an audit of 188 debt collection cases granted default judgment by the Superior Court of Los Angeles, we find that 4% contained major defects that should have entirely prevented default judgment, 10% contained inconsistencies requiring reduced judgments, and 32% contained errors requiring amendment prior to judgment. To support courthouses in default judgment review, we collaborated with courthouse attorneys and judges in designing a Default Assistant. The Default Assistant employs large language models to evaluate a case with respect to predetermined legal requirements and provide cited recommendations for an expert user's review. We equip users to verify these recommendations by grounding the assistant's explanations in cited quotes and tables from the original case filings. We conduct a controlled study with 66 law students that conservatively simulates court review, with more time and resources than court staff. We nevertheless find users aided by the Default Assistant were 6.0% more accurate on the average requirement than unaided reviewers (p < 1.0e-4). Simultaneously, users were 25.9% faster in reviewing the average requirement than unaided reviewers (p < 2.5e-10). Statutory requirements demanding extensive document search realized the largest gains, with error reductions and time savings from AI assistance up to 62% and 34%, respectively, relative to unassisted user performance and with differences statistically significant (p < 0.05). Our work provides a proof-of-concept that AI assistants with citations have the potential to help resource-constrained courts conduct default judgment review more accurately and efficiently.

</details>


### [499] [Artificial Intelligence-Enabled Accounting Information Systems and Fraud Detection in Nigeria's Financial Services Sector: The Moderating Role of Natural Language Processing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01257)
*Timothy Oluwapelumi Adeyemi,Abigail Omotola Ojogbede*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The study explores the effectiveness of AI-enabled AIS and the moderating role of NLP in improving fraud detection and auditing in Nigeria's financial sector.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the shortcomings of conventional auditing systems in combating sophisticated financial crimes in a rapidly digitalising financial environment.

Method: Used a quantitative cross-sectional survey of 186 professionals in Nigeria's banking, insurance, and FinTech sectors. Analyses involved descriptive statistics, multiple regression, and moderated regression techniques.

Result: AI-enabled AIS significantly enhances auditing and fraud detection, with NLP playing a role in improving effectiveness by enabling better semantic analysis and explainability.

Conclusion: AI-enabled AIS and NLP are vital tools for improving fraud governance, regulatory compliance, and institutional trust in digital financial settings.

Abstract: The rapid digitalisation of financial systems has improved operational efficiency and financial inclusion while simultaneously increasing exposure to sophisticated forms of cyber-enabled fraud and electronic financial misconduct. Conventional auditing systems, which largely depend on retrospective verification and rule-based monitoring, increasingly struggle to address the complexity and speed of modern financial crime. Consequently, financial institutions are progressively adopting Artificial Intelligence (AI)-enabled Accounting Information Systems (AIS) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) technologies to strengthen fraud detection, continuous auditing, and institutional monitoring. This study examined the influence of AI-enabled AIS on auditing and fraud detection effectiveness within Nigeria's financial services sector while additionally evaluating the moderating role of NLP. Anchored on the Fraud Diamond Theory and the Technology Acceptance Model, the study adopted a quantitative cross-sectional survey design. Primary data were collected from 186 professionals across banking, insurance, and FinTech institutions in Nigeria. Data were analysed using descriptive statistics, multiple regression, and hierarchical moderated regression techniques. The findings revealed that AI-enabled AIS significantly improves auditing and fraud detection effectiveness, particularly through prevention, detection, data analysis, and investigative capabilities. The results further indicated that NLP positively moderates the relationship between AI-enabled AIS and auditing effectiveness by improving semantic interpretation and analytical explainability. The study concludes that AI-enabled AIS and NLP are increasingly important for strengthening fraud governance, regulatory accountability, and institutional trust within emerging digital financial environments.

</details>


### [500] [The Rising Unsustainability of AI Graphics Cards Production](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01258)
*Clément Morand,Aurélie Névéol,Anne-Laure Ligozat*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The study examines the environmental damages of graphics cards production, emphasizing the rising impact from 2013 to 2025 and the need for transparency and sufficiency in AI practices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the understudied aspect of environmental damages from graphics card production, vital to AI training.

Method: The authors compile and analyze a dataset on NVIDIA workstation graphics cards production, evaluating impacts on energy use, carbon emissions, and resource depletion from 2013 to 2025.

Result: The research indicates a steady rise in production-related environmental damages of graphics cards over the analyzed period.

Conclusion: The study calls for transparency in life-cycle assessments and advocates structural changes in AI practices, including policy measures and longer-lasting hardware designs, to address mounting environmental impacts.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has been accompanied by significant increases in computational and environmental costs, driven by large-scale investments in AI infrastructure, hardware, and software. In particular, graphics cards have become central to AI training, with frequent hardware updates required to meet escalating computational demands. However, the environmental damages of graphics cards production remain understudied.
  This study addresses this gap by estimating the environmental damages associated with graphics cards production over the past decade (2013-2025). We analyze trends in energy consumption, carbon emissions and resource depletion.
  We compile and provide a dataset documenting the environmental damages of NVIDIA workstation graphics cards production since 2013. Our analysis of this dataset reveals a steady increase in production-related impacts over the period.
  Our finding highlights the need for greater transparency in life-cycle data, a persistent challenge in AI environmental assessments. While operational efficiency improvements (e.g., energy-efficient training, carbon-aware computing) are often prioritized, our results underscore that production-related impacts are also escalating and cannot be overlooked.
  The AI community must move beyond incremental optimizations and confront the necessity of sufficiency. This shift may demand structural changes such as policy interventions, hardware design for longevity, and cultural shifts away from perpetual growth and increased performance.

</details>


### [501] [AI Virtue: What is "Good" Knowledge in the Age of Artificial Intelligence?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01776)
*Alan Liu*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper examines what constitutes valuable knowledge in the AI era, using digital humanities methods to analyze epistemic virtues in AI-related journal articles, particularly focusing on creativity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore how AI challenges traditional epistemic values and propose a framework for evaluating AI's generative knowledge-worth.

Method: Digital humanities techniques were employed to analyze epistemic virtues in a corpus of 553 journal articles on AI from 2024, with a focus on the concept of creativity.

Result: The study provides a discourse analysis on values like creativity, suggesting a move away from pre-AI values towards future-oriented generativity.

Conclusion: A new evaluative framework for knowledge in the AI era is proposed, emphasizing generative values while providing a digital toolkit for further exploration.

Abstract: In the age of AI, what will be good knowledge? This article, which is accepted and forthcoming in a special issue of Modern Fiction Studies on "Cultural AI" in 2027, applies digital humanities methods to map epistemic virtues (like "true," "accurate," "creative") used in a corpus of 553 journal articles on AI published in 2024. "Creativity" comes in for special attention as an example. Exploring this discourse of value, the article considers how a framework might be developed for evaluating the knowledge-worth of AI -- one less locked into values formed around pre-AI "knowledge work" agents or structures, and more open to the future values of "generativity." The essay is supported by an online digital kit for exploring data models of the corpus of articles on AI it studies.

</details>


### [502] [Overview of Risk Assessment and Management for Intelligent Systems under the AI Act and Beyond](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02197)
*Javier Irigoyen,Roberto Daza,Aythami Morales,Julian Fierrez,Ruben Tolosana,Ruben Vera-Rodriguez,Francisco Jurado,Alvaro Ortigosa*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper reviews AI risk assessment methodologies and highlights gaps for future research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emerging regulatory frameworks and societal concerns necessitate better understanding and management of risks in AI systems.

Method: The study reviews worldwide regulations, characterizes AI risks, and evaluates existing methodologies, focusing on general frameworks.

Result: Identified best practices and gaps in AI risk assessment methodologies, emphasizing areas needing further research.

Conclusion: There is a need for advancing systematic AI risk assessment methodologies and addressing identified gaps to ensure ethical and safe AI development.

Abstract: The society and emerging risk-based regulatory frameworks for AI underscore the need for rigorous risk assessment to ensure safe and reliable AI systems. In response to this imperative, this paper presents an overview of AI risk assessment (identification and analysis) and management methodologies. It begins by reviewing the worldwide regulatory landscape that drives the need for systematic AI risk assessment. Then we characterize the spectrum of AI-related risks identified in the literature, from technical failures to ethical and social impacts. Subsequently, it reviews key risk assessment methodologies proposed for AI systems, focusing on general frameworks. The paper highlights best practices and illuminates methodological gaps, highlighting areas for further research on AI risk assessment.

</details>


### [503] [The Eticas AI Risk Taxonomy: Open Infrastructure for Operationalizing AI Audits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02201)
*Gemma Galdon Clavell,Pablo Accuosto,Usman Gohar*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper addresses the fragmentation in AI risk evaluation by presenting a robust operationalized approach to auditing AI systems, using a standardized taxonomy and risk testing method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI systems deployed in critical domains require standardized auditing methods that go beyond simply identifying risks, moving towards actionable tests and evaluations.

Method: The authors introduce Eticas AI Risk Taxonomy v2.0.0, which operationalizes risk evaluation through standardized auditing, exemplifying it via a graded test on GPT-4-0314 for PII leakage.

Result: Using the Eticas methodology, risk evaluation shows disclosure rates of 0%, 51%, and 84% under varying adversarial conditions, with a subcategory grade of E and SYSTEMIC risk pattern.

Conclusion: The paper contributes a scalable, open AI auditing framework that bridges concept and actionable findings for effective risk assessment and compliance in high-stakes domains.

Abstract: The rapid deployment of AI systems across high-stakes domains has created urgent demand for standardized evaluation, yet the field remains fragmented across competing risk taxonomies that catalog risks without showing how an audit is executed. At least 74 AI risk taxonomies exist, and almost all stop at the catalog. The hard part of auditing is not naming a risk but operationalizing it: turning it into a test run against a real system, a measured value, a calibrated severity, and a defensible grade. This paper leads with that bridge. We present the operationalization layer Eticas has built and run, shown end to end on a single risk (PII leakage) against a public benchmark, and then the open taxonomy that makes the method scale. On GPT-4-0314, a disclosure risk that seven external frameworks require be controlled is measured at 0%, 51%, and 84% disclosure as adversarial conditioning increases, mapping through calibrated severity bands to a subcategory grade of E with a SYSTEMIC pattern. Around this example, the Eticas AI Risk Taxonomy v2.0.0 organizes 76 active subcategories across 10 categories and 20 sub-groups, with mappings to 18 external frameworks across compliance, reference, and academic tiers. Its category and sub-group layer is published under CC BY 4.0 as open semantic infrastructure with stable URIs and SKOS/JSON-LD distributions, and a worked subcategory example shows the operational layer down to its severity thresholds. The contribution is the demonstrated bridge from concept to graded finding, anchored by a clean separation of risks from the mechanisms by which they surface, and framed by an open-core model in which the conceptual scaffold is open and the methodology calibration is the practitioner layer. This is the infrastructure the AI auditing field needs: shared, open, and demonstrably operable.

</details>


### [504] [Human Capital, Not Model Benchmarks, Predicts Hybrid Intelligence in Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02467)
*Vivienne Ming*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: Human-AI collaboration's success is not universal and depends on specific human traits such as perspective-taking, intellectual humility, and curiosity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether pairing humans with AI improves outcomes and identify factors influencing effective human-AI collaboration.

Method: Used a real-money prediction market as a benchmark to analyze individual forecaster performance when collaborating with AI.

Result: Hybrid performance showed three outcomes: matching the model, performing worse than the model, or exceeding the market benchmark when complementary reasoning occurred.

Conclusion: Traits like perspective-taking, humility, and curiosity predict effective collaboration, while raw cognitive ability or model benchmarks do not.

Abstract: Whether pairing people with AI helps or hurts is usually reported as a single average effect. Using a real-money prediction market (Polymarket) as an objective, externally resolved benchmark, this pilot shows that the value of human-AI collaboration depends on a specific, measurable form of human capital. Analyzed at the level of the individual forecaster, hybrid performance is trimodal: most people either deferred to the model (matching it) or used it to rubber-stamp a prior guess (performing worse than the model alone), while a minority engaged in genuine complementary reasoning and reached accuracy matching or even exceeding (i.e., lower error than) the market itself. Collaborative traits (perspective-taking, intellectual humility, and curiosity) rather than raw cognitive ability or model benchmarks, distinguished who reached that mode. The results are preliminary but statistically robust, and motivate a pre-registered replication now in preparation.

</details>


<div id='eess.AS'></div>

# eess.AS [[Back]](#toc)

### [505] [Self-Supervised Test-Time Tuning for Packet Loss Concealment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01823)
*Yehoshua Dissen,Joseph Keshet*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: This paper introduces TTT-PLC, a self-supervised framework that dynamically adapts Packet Loss Concealment (PLC) models during deployment using only received audio packets, improving concealment without requiring clean references or external data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the inefficiency of fixed-parameter PLC models by leveraging the real-time information available during deployment to improve signal reconstruction.

Method: TTT-PLC works by synthetically masking parts of the received audio signal to create training data on the fly, then using the PLC objective to tune the model parameters in real-time, adapting them specifically to the current signal.

Result: The framework was tested on different PLC backbones (FRN and PARCnet), demonstrating improvements in audio reconstruction quality without requiring modifications to the original models or external data.

Conclusion: TTT-PLC effectively showcases that received lossy signals can train and improve PLC models during deployment, challenging the traditionally static nature of these models and providing an adaptive runtime solution.

Abstract: Packet loss concealment (PLC) reconstructs audio packets that are missing at the receiver, usually with a trained model whose parameters remain fixed at deployment time. This treats the PLC model as static, even though each call or recording exposes signal-specific information through the packets that did arrive. We present TTT-PLC, a self-supervised test-time tuning framework that adapts existing PLC models using only those received packets. The method creates supervision by synthetically masking portions of the available signal, training the model to conceal them with its native PLC objective, and then using the adapted model to reconstruct the true packet losses. No clean reference signal, external adaptation data, or architectural modification is required.
  We study TTT-PLC in two deployment settings. In the non-causal setting, the received file is available before reconstruction, allowing repeated self-supervised adaptation passes and providing a per-file adaptation ceiling. In the causal setting, audio is streamed without revising emitted samples; adaptation is performed only on completed past blocks, and updated parameters affect only future audio. We instantiate the framework on two public PLC backbones, FRN, a recurrent full-band speech PLC model, and PARCnet, a hybrid autoregressive-neural model for networked music. Across these settings, the results show that pretrained PLC systems do not need to be treated as fixed at inference time, the still-observed portions of a lossy signal can provide an effective training signal for improving concealment on that same signal.

</details>


### [506] [CNN Models for Microphone Array Covariance Matrix Upsampling and Acoustic Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01295)
*Marianthi Adamopoulou,Parthasaarathy Sudarsanam,David Diaz-Guerra,Meng Jiang,Archontis Politis,Seyed Jalaleddin Mousavirad,Tuomas Virtanen,Jan Lundgren*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: This paper develops deep learning methods to virtually augment a 4-microphone array to a 32-microphone array for acoustic imaging tasks, improving spatial resolution without extra hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limited sensor availability and the need for enhanced spatial resolution in acoustic imaging systems without increasing hardware complexity motivate this study.

Method: The study employs deep learning with five neural network architectures, leveraging 2D convolutional layers and frequency dynamic convolution, to upsample a 4-microphone covariance matrix to a 32-microphone matrix. These methods are trained on the STARSS23 dataset.

Result: The best-performing model achieves a root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.432, outperforming the baseline RMSE of 0.548. Beamforming heatmap visualizations confirm the models produce results comparable to a physical 32-microphone array.

Conclusion: Deep learning-based covariance upsampling effectively enhances the acoustic imaging performance of a 4-microphone array, offering a cost-effective alternative to increasing hardware complexity.

Abstract: Acoustic imaging visualization is a core methodology in acoustics, enabling spatial analysis of sound sources and acoustic scenes. However, limited sensor availability in practical systems motivate approaches that enhance spatial resolution without increasing the hardware complexity. In this paper, we focus on upsampling virtually a tetrahedral 4-microphone array to a spherical 32-microphone array by estimating the covariance matrices of the channels employing deep learning techniques. Five neural network architectures are investigated for covariance upsampling for acoustic imaging using the real-world STARSS23 dataset. These models are developed to estimate a 32-microphone, time-frequency covariance matrix from a 4-microphone input covariance representation. The proposed architectures are based on 2D convolutional layers to capture the underlying spatial-spectral structure of covariance matrices, and are further enhanced with frequency dynamic convolution to model their frequency-dependent properties. The proposed architectures are evaluated in terms of root mean square error (RMSE) and using delay-and-sum beamforming acoustic imaging. Quantitative results show that all models outperform a random-guess baseline, which yields an RMSE of 0.548, with the best-performing architecture achieving an RMSE of 0.432. We analyze qualitatively the performance of the proposed models through beamforming heatmap visualizations derived from the 4-channel input covariance, the 32-channel ground truth, and the predicted 32-channel covariance matrices. These results demonstrate that covariance upsampling significantly enhances the effective performance of the 4-channel microphone array, producing sound maps that closely resemble those obtained with the 32-channel array.

</details>


### [507] [Few-Shot Open-Set Audio Classification Using Attention Information-Fused Prototypes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01297)
*Yanxiong Li,Jiaxin Tan,Qianqian Li,Guoqing Chen,Sen Huang,Tuomas Virtanen*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The paper introduces Few-shot Open-set Audio Classification (FOAC), a method to classify seen audio classes while rejecting unseen ones, outperforming prior approaches in accuracy and computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing audio classification methods fail to reject unseen class samples and mistakenly classify them as seen classes. The motivation is to address this limitation for broader applicability.

Method: The proposed FOAC method uses a model comprising a ResNet encoder for embeddings and a classifier with prototype generators for both few-shot and open-set classes. It employs weighted prototypes fused from class-discriminative features and meta-training techniques on public datasets.

Result: Experiments on three datasets (LS-100, NSynth-100, FSC-89) demonstrate superior performance in AUROC and accuracy over existing methods, with statistical significance and lower computational complexity.

Conclusion: FOAC provides a highly effective solution for audio classification that addresses the open-set challenge, balancing computational efficiency and classification accuracy. The implementation code is available for replication.

Abstract: Most existing audio classification methods suppose that each query (testing) sample belongs to a class of support (training) samples, and misrecognize samples of unseen classes as seen classes (cannot reject samples of unseen classes). In this study, we propose a method for Few-shot Open-set Audio Classification (FOAC), which can recognize query samples of seen classes after updating the model using a few support samples, and meanwhile reject query samples from unseen classes. We design a model consisting of an encoder and a classifier. The encoder is the backbone of a ResNet used for extracting embeddings. The classifier consists of prototype generators of few-shot classes and open-set classes. Prototypes of few-shot classes are obtained by fusing the class-discriminative information of support and query embeddings and by assigning larger weighting coefficient to representative part of the support embeddings. One prototype is generated for open-set classes using the proposed prototype generator. The encoder is trained with abundant samples of base classes in supervised manner, and then the prototypes of base classes are generated under the supervision of a joint loss. The classifier is trained using a few samples of few-shot classes in a meta-training way. Three public datasets (LS-100, NSynth-100, and FSC-89) are used to assess the performance of our method. Experiments show that our method has advantage over prior methods in AUROC and accuracy. This advantage has statistical significance for most prior methods. Our method has lower computational complexity than most prior methods. The code is at https://github.com/Jessytan/FOAC-AIFP.

</details>


### [508] [An Efficient vLLM-Based Inference Pipeline for Unified Audio Understanding and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02119)
*Haoran Wang,Jinchuan Tian,Siddhant Arora,Shinji Watanabe*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The paper introduces a vLLM-based inference pipeline for efficient and unified speech understanding and generation, addressing performance bottlenecks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing high-throughput inference engines struggle with native multimodal generation support, especially in Speech Language Models, due to incompatibilities with standard single-stream inference architectures.

Method: The paper proposes an extension to autoregressive decoding that supports delay-pattern de-interleaving, coordinated multi-stream sampling, and an on-GPU acoustic decoder for waveform synthesis. A novel CFG implementation allows for sustained throughput while handling conditional and unconditional requests.

Result: The framework sustains 80% of non-CFG throughput while overcoming bottlenecks introduced by standard multimodal processing workflows. The approach is optimized for high performance and native multimodal generation.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that it is possible to achieve efficient speech model inference through novel architectural adjustments, setting a benchmark for future unified multimodal frameworks. The framework is made open source for community use.

Abstract: While Large Multimodal Models excel in comprehension, high-throughput inference engines lack native support for multimodal generation. This is severe in Speech Language Models, where generating multi-layered audio tokens via decoupled AR+NAR or synchronous Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) with delay-pattern interleaving conflicts with standard single-stream loops. We present a vLLM-based inference pipeline for unified speech understanding and generation. We extend autoregressive decoding to natively execute delay-pattern de-interleaving and coordinated multi-stream sampling, integrating an on-GPU acoustic decoder for end-to-end waveform synthesis. Crucially, we overcome the shared intuition that Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) halves throughput. By co-scheduling paired conditional and unconditional requests within a continuous batch, our CFG implementation sustains 80% of non-CFG throughput, absorbing dual-request and logit merging overheads. We open-source our framework.

</details>


<div id='cs.DB'></div>

# cs.DB [[Back]](#toc)

### [509] [AgenticDataBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Data Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01647)
*Zhaoyan Sun,Shan Zhong,Daizhou Wen,Jiaxing Han,Guoliang Li,Ying Yan,Peng Zhang,Yu Su,Xiang Qi,Baolin Sun,Chengyuan Yang,Tao Fang,Huaiyu Ruan*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: AgenticDataBench is a new benchmark designed to evaluate large language model-based data agents comprehensively across diverse and realistic data science workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to automate data science workflows to reduce labor intensity and enable scalability, coupled with a lack of comprehensive benchmarks for evaluating AI agents in diverse data science contexts.

Method: AgenticDataBench introduces a diverse set of tasks from 15 domains, incorporates real-world data, introduces skill-based task quantification, and generates tasks using hierarchical clustering and LLM methods.

Result: It provides detailed skill-level insights into the performance of cutting-edge data agents by applying this benchmark and opens an annotated, open-sourced testbed.

Conclusion: AgenticDataBench addresses the existing evaluation gap for LLM-based data agents, offering a realistic, diverse, and fine-grained framework for performance assessment.

Abstract: Data science aims to derive actionable insights from heterogeneous raw data, unlocking the value of the massive amounts of data generated in modern society. Automating this process is essential to reducing labor-intensive efforts for data scientists and enabling scalable data-driven applications. Recently, large language model (LLM)-based data agents have emerged as a promising solution to automate data science workflows. However, the field lacks comprehensive benchmarks to rigorously evaluate these agents across diverse scenarios with fine-grained granularity. To address this gap, we propose AgenticDataBench, a comprehensive benchmark featuring realistic tasks spanning diverse domains with fine-grained ground-truth labels. This enables evaluations to capture the diversity and complexity of data science workflows and the detailed performance of agents. First, to cover diverse domains, we collect real datasets and tasks from 15 vertical domains, including 5 real-world B2B use cases from a leading fintech company. Second, to remove redundancy in real-world tasks and generate high-quality tasks for domains lacking real data, we introduce data science skills, recurring data-centric operational patterns, and quantify benchmark coverage by the number of skills included. Representative skills are extracted from large-scale task solutions on Stack Overflow using skill-aligned hierarchical clustering. Third, for real-world business tasks, we select task-solution pairs that maximize diversity in skill composition, ensuring broad coverage of practical scenarios. Fourth, to generate realistic tasks for devise domains without real tasks, we propose a systematic LLM-based task generation approach to create workflows and tasks based on these skills. Finally, we evaluate state-of-the-art data agents using our annotated benchmark and open-sourced testbed, providing detailed skill-level insights.

</details>


### [510] [HNSW with Accuracy Guarantees Using Graph Spanners -- A Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02338)
*Minghao Li,Raghav Mittal,Sanjivni Rana,Suraj Shetiya,Gautam Das,Nick Koudas*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel 'Certify-then-Rectify' framework to enhance HNSW graph retrieval by combining heuristic speed with exact retrieval rigor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HNSW graphs are widely used for efficient search but lack theoretical guarantees. The authors aim to address this gap by ensuring both speed and correctness in search operations.

Method: A framework that certifies HNSW search quality using statistical checks and escalates to exact recovery when necessary, leveraging geometric spanners and Extreme Value Theory for computational feasibility.

Result: The proposed framework achieves the speed of HNSW in average cases while ensuring correctness and surpasses other approaches in benchmark evaluations.

Conclusion: The approach effectively combines HNSW’s efficiency with exact search reliability, offering a step forward in graph-based search methodologies.

Abstract: Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) graphs serve as the industry standard due to their logarithmic complexity and strong empirical performance. However, HNSW relies on greedy graph traversal, a heuristic that provides no theoretical guarantees of correctness. In this paper, we propose a novel "Certify-then-Rectify" framework that bridges the gap between the speed of heuristic search and the rigor of exact retrieval. Rather than discarding HNSW, our approach first employs a distribution-free statistical certifier to dynamically evaluate the quality of a standard HNSW search with minimal overhead. If certification indicates that the retrieved neighbors are of low quality, the framework safely escalates to a rigorous exact recovery algorithm. To make this exact recovery computationally feasible, we reinterpret the HNSW graph as a geometric spanner and utilize Extreme Value Theory to stochastically estimate its maximum empirical stretch factor. This allows us to mathematically bound the maximum distance of true nearest neighbors. Extensive evaluations on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our tiered framework delivers the average-case speed of HNSW while ensuring the worst-case correctness of exact search and outperforming other applicable approaches.

</details>


<div id='math.LO'></div>

# math.LO [[Back]](#toc)

### [511] [Fast approximation and learning of binary classification tasks in o-minimal structures using ReLU neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01266)
*Clemens Kinn,Philipp Petersen*

Main category: math.LO

TL;DR: The paper examines using ReLU neural networks to approximate binary classification problems with decision sets defined by o-minimal expansions. It provides approximation rates and statistical learning insights for such definable functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is motivated by the need to understand how well ReLU neural networks can approximate decision regions defined by o-minimal structures, which are grounded in mathematical logic and geometry, extending applicability to structured, complex functions.

Method: The authors introduce the concept of "traceable sets" to represent definable decision regions and analyze their $L^p$ approximation through ReLU neural networks. They derive explicit approximation rates based on the complexity of these sets and boundaries. They further use entropy estimates to assess statistical learning rates.

Result: The paper demonstrates that ReLU networks can approximate characteristic functions of traceable sets within a defined error bound on $[-1/2,1/2]^n$, achieving size $ackslash mathcal{O}( varepsilon^{-p(n-1)/m})$. This approximation is independent of depth and uses polynomially bounded weights. The study also provides statistical learning rates for classifiers built via empirical risk minimization.

Conclusion: This research establishes a connection between o-minimal expansion, cell decomposition, and ReLU networks in approximating definable sets. It paves the way for using deep learning within structured mathematical frameworks, achieving reliable approximation with provable statistical performance.

Abstract: We study binary classification problems whose decision sets are given by definable sets in o-minimal expansions of the real field. Motivated by cell decomposition of definable sets, we introduce traceable sets as a classical proxy for definable decision regions and analyze their approximation by ReLU neural networks. Under uniform bounds on the number of connected components and suitable $C^m$ extensions for the boundary functions, we prove that characteristic functions of traceable subsets of $[-1/2,1/2]^n$ can be approximated in $L^p$ to accuracy $\varepsilon>0$ by ReLU neural networks of size $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-p(n-1)/m})$, with depth independent of $\varepsilon$ and polynomially bounded weights. This establishes quantitative approximation rates for certain definable collections in o-minimal structures using ReLU neural networks. The same approach also yields the stated approximation rates for a subclass of definable maps $[-1/2,1/2]^n \to \mathbb{R}$. We then combine the approximation capabilities with entropy estimates for ReLU neural network classes to obtain statistical learning rates for empirical risk minimization with hinge loss. For $N$ uniformly distributed samples, the resulting classifiers achieve expected misclassification error of order $N^{-m/(m+pn-p)}$ up to an arbitrarily small polynomial loss.

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.quant-gas'></div>

# cond-mat.quant-gas [[Back]](#toc)

### [512] [Q-GAIN: A Python Package for Machine Learning and Physically Informed Analysis Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02413)
*M. Doris,S. Guo,S. M. Koh,L. Ritter,A. R. Fritsch,S. Mukherjee,I. B. Spielman,J. P. Zwolak*

Main category: cond-mat.quant-gas

TL;DR: Q-GAIN is a Python package designed for rapid application of machine learning and physics-informed techniques in cold-atom experiments, particularly focusing on images of Bose-Einstein condensates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to create a versatile tool for researchers to integrate machine learning and physics-based analysis for enhanced evaluation of cold-atom experimental data.

Method: Q-GAIN applies modular workflows, combining data preprocessing, machine learning for feature extraction (classification, object detection), and physics-based analysis routines.

Result: The framework was demonstrated by implementing ML tasks like handwritten digit classification, soliton detection, and vortex identification in BEC images.

Conclusion: Q-GAIN provides a flexible and expandable framework for analyzing cold-atom experiments, emphasizing modularity and applicability in various research contexts.

Abstract: Here we describe the quantum gas analysis and inference (Q-GAIN) Python package, which enables rapid deployment of machine learning (ML) and physics-informed analysis techniques for cold-atom experiments. Out of the box, Q-GAIN implements classification, object detection, and physics-informed metrics for feature detection in images of atomic Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs). Q-GAIN encourages a natural, module-based workflow: starting with data loading and preprocessing, followed by ML-based feature identification, and ending with conventional analysis techniques. We demonstrate this modularity by configuring Q-GAIN for three ML tasks. First, we demonstrate the basic workflow of the Q-GAIN framework by implementing the standard task of classifying handwritten digits from the MNIST dataset. Then, we re-implement our earlier soliton detection (SolDet) package in the Q-GAIN framework, enabling the detection and analysis of solitonic excitations in time-of-flight data. Finally, we develop an object-detection tool that identifies quantized vortices in images of ring-shaped BECs.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [513] [Simulation Based Reward Function Validation for Multi-Agent On Orbit Inspection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01367)
*Patrick Quinn,Bala Prenith Reddy Gopu,George M. Nehma,Madhur Tiwari*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper introduces a generalized reward function for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) in spacecraft inspection tasks, enabling flexible imaging decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve spacecraft inspection by developing a more flexible MARL model, as prior work focused solely on predefined inspection points.

Method: Generalized reward functions based on 3D object reconstructions are introduced, allowing agents decision-making over image collection timing and locations.

Result: Agents trained with this model provide insights into optimizing MARL for inspection tasks and broader inspection strategies.

Conclusion: The approach enhances MARL's adaptability and offers guidance for spacecraft inspection beyond MARL applications.

Abstract: A proposed method for the control of groups of inspection spacecraft is Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL). While MARL has already been employed for this purpose in previous work, the reward functions used focus on reaching a finite set of predetermined inspection points around the target. In this work, we study and develop a generalized reward function for the MARL inspection task informed by the analysis of 3D reconstructions of inspected objects in orbit. Because the reward function is generalized such that any number of images at arbitrary locations may evaluated, we also allow trained agents to have complete control over when images are collected. With this approach, we gather insights into best practices for not only the specific MARL inspection task, but also gain key takeaways informative to the broader inspection task outside of a MARL context.

</details>


### [514] [CausalSteward: An Agentic Divide-Conquer-Combine Copilot for Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01936)
*Nicholas Tagliapietra,Gian Lorenzo Marchioni,Moritz Willig,Juergen Luettin,Lavdim Halilaj,Kristian Kersting*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: CausalSTeward (CAST) is a human-in-the-loop system designed to integrate prior knowledge with high-dimensional causal discovery using a collaborative and divide-and-conquer approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address challenges in learning causal models from high-dimensional data, particularly when real-world assumptions are violated, and existing prior knowledge is underutilized.

Method: The study introduces CAST, a multi-agent collaborative framework that iteratively partitions large variable clusters for analysis, combining prior knowledge and data-driven techniques such as retrieval augmented generation and conditional independence tests.

Result: CAST successfully combines prior knowledge with computational methods for causal discovery, improving analysis of high-dimensional data and exploring the boundaries of causal reasoning in collaborative systems.

Conclusion: The paper highlights the importance of human-in-the-loop frameworks in achieving trustworthy and accurate causal modeling, showcasing the potential of CAST in overcoming high-dimensional causality challenges.

Abstract: Learning causal models from high-dimensional data is a significant challenge, particularly in real-world settings where violations of core assumptions lead to causal identifiability issues. Although massive amounts of prior knowledge are available, and contain valuable causal information, effectively integrating this knowledge into the causal discovery process remains an open problem. We introduce CausalSTeward (CAST), a novel human-in-the-loop framework for interactively assembling large causal models. CausalSteward is a multi-agent collaborative system that tackles high-dimensional causality through a divide-and-conquer approach where large clusters of variables are iteratively partitioned and then separately analyzed. Our framework fuses prior knowledge with a data-driven approach by using tailored tools such as retrieval augmented generation and conditional independence tests. Finally, we use this work to examine the capabilities and limitations of causal reasoning in multi-agent frameworks, and how the human-in-the-loop can contribute to accurate and trustworthy results.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [515] [ExPerT: Personalizing LLM Responses to Users' Domain Expertise via Query-Wise Semantic and Keystroke Behavioral Cues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01242)
*Yeji Park,Jiwon Tark,Taesik Gong*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces ExPerT, a personalization framework for large language models (LLMs) that tailors responses based on a user's query-specific expertise using semantic and behavioral cues, showcasing significant improvement in expertise inference accuracy and user satisfaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing personalization methods fail to account for query-specific variations in user expertise, necessitating a new approach to improve LLM adaptability in response generation.

Method: ExPerT combines a semantic-behavioral expertise inference module and expertise-conditioned response generation to analyze and adapt LLM outputs to user's domain expertise. It involves in-context LLM prompting through semantic and keystroke dynamics.

Result: In a user study with 40 participants and 1270 queries, ExPerT outperformed baseline methods by reducing expertise inference error by 65.7% and increasing response satisfaction by 17.52%.

Conclusion: ExPerT effectively personalizes LLM responses by leveraging both semantic and behavioral cues, demonstrating an enhanced ability to adapt output quality to user expertise levels.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used by end users, yet existing personalization methods relying on static profiles or text-only signals fail to capture query-specific expertise variation. We present ExPerT, a query-wise personalization framework that adapts LLM responses to users' query domain expertise by combining semantic and behavioral cues. ExPerT consists of two key components: (i) a semantic-behavioral expertise inference module that jointly interprets query text and keystroke dynamics via in-context LLM prompting, and (ii) an expertise-conditioned response generation that adapts the level of detail, terminology, and conceptual complexity. Our user study with 40 participants and 1270 queries demonstrated that ExPerT reduced expertise inference error by 65.7% compared to the strongest baseline (MAE = 0.398 vs. 1.162) and improved response satisfaction by 17.52% (from 3.71 to 4.36) on a 5-point Likert scale.

</details>


### [516] [What Types of Human-AI Teams Exist?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02198)
*Nathan Hughes,Ibrahim Habli*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper categorizes Human-AI teaming research into five clusters to address inconsistencies in the field, offering insights into transferring knowledge across papers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the inconsistency and lack of clarity in the categorization of Human-AI teams across various domains.

Method: The researchers analyze 53 papers on Human-AI teams, categorizing them into five clusters based on psychological teaming taxonomies.

Result: Identified five distinct clusters: AI Assistant, Ad-hoc Dependency, Ad-hoc Forced Dependency, Paired Equanimity, and Group Equanimity.

Conclusion: The paper provides a typology framework, reporting checklist, and recommendations to improve synthesis across Human-AI teaming research.

Abstract: Human-AI teaming has received increasing attention in the literature. However, the range of studies conducted in multiple domains make it difficult to understand what types of teams are being studied, and in what ways are they similar/different from one another. In this study, we analyse 53 papers on human-AI teams and categorise them into five main clusters based on psychological taxonomies of teaming; AI Assistant, Ad-hoc Dependency, Ad-hoc Forced Dependency, Paired Equanimity, and Group Equanimity. Each cluster represents a unique combination of holistic team-level characteristics, indicating there are multiple disparate team types studied under the same definition. In turn, this raises the question of whether insights are truly transferable between papers. We conclude with guidance on how to identify the types of human-AI teams studied, a checklist for reporting a human-AI team in research work, and ways in which the field can be further synthesised.

</details>


<div id='cs.DS'></div>

# cs.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [517] [Scalable and Distributed Silhouette Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01993)
*Ilie Sarpe,Federico Altieri,Andrea Pietracaprina,Geppino Pucci,Fabio Vandin*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: The authors propose rigorous sampling-based algorithms to efficiently estimate local and global silhouette scores for $k$-clustering, reducing complexity from $Θ(n^2)$ to $O(nk\varepsilon^{-2}\ln(nk/δ))$ distance calculations while maintaining provable accuracy guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation arises from the prohibitively high computational cost of exact silhouette score calculations ($Θ(n^2)$), especially for modern large-scale datasets, and the lack of provable guarantees in existing approximation methods.

Method: The proposed methods use sampling to reduce computation, achieving $O(nk\varepsilon^{-2}\ln(nk/δ))$ complexity. Parameters $\varepsilon$ and $δ$ allow control over result accuracy and efficiency. Additionally, the approach is extended to distributed frameworks like MapReduce and MPC with sublinear memory and minimal communication rounds.

Result: Their approach delivers the best accuracy-efficiency trade-off compared to state-of-the-art methods, effectively scaling to massive datasets where exact silhouette computation is infeasible.

Conclusion: The introduced algorithms significantly improve silhouette estimation efficiency for both local and global tasks while providing provable accuracy guarantees, making them practical for large-scale clustering analysis.

Abstract: The silhouette is one of the most widely used measures to assess the quality of a $k$-clustering of a dataset of $n$ elements. Its evaluation requires no information beyond the clustering assignment. In addition, the silhouette is extremely easy to interpret, providing a score to measure the quality of a clustering as a whole or for each element. The exact computation of the: (i) silhouette of each element of a dataset; and (ii) the global silhouette of the clustering; require $Θ(n^2)$ distance calculations, under general metrics. The quadratic complexity $Θ(n^2)$ is extremely prohibitive, especially on massive modern datasets. Surprisingly, existing approximate methods using $O(n^2)$ distance calculations are heuristics not offering provable and controllable guarantees on the quality of their results.
  We introduce the first rigorous and efficient algorithms to estimate: (i) the (local) silhouette of each element of a dataset; and (ii) the (global) silhouette; of any metric $k$-clustering. Our methods, based on sampling, perform $O(nk\varepsilon^{-2}\ln (nk/δ))$ distance computations, and provide estimates with additive error $O(\varepsilon)$ with probability at least $1-δ$. That is, parameters $\varepsilon$ and $δ$ in $(0,1)$ control the trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. We also introduce a scalable and distributed design of our methods for the MapReduce and Massively Parallel Computing (MPC) frameworks. Our distributed algorithms use a constant number of rounds and sublinear local memory. Finally, we perform extensive experiments against state-of-the-art approaches. The results show that our new techniques yield the best trade-off between accuracy and efficiency for both local and global silhouette estimation. In addition, our methods scale efficiently to massive datasets for which an exact computation of the silhouette is not practical.

</details>


### [518] [Tight Lower Bounds for the Multi-Secretary Problem via Bellman Certificates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02150)
*Jiawei Zhang*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: This paper establishes that in multi-secretary problems with bounded-density distributions and support gaps, the additive regret has a lower bound of \(O((\log T)^2)\), matching known upper bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether the additional logarithmic factor in regret bounds for distributions with support gaps is necessary.

Method: The paper utilizes Bellman certificates, a framework involving relaxations of the Bellman recursion, to construct lower bounds and analyze conditions leading to higher regret.

Result: The work proves that the optimal regret for some gapped distributions, like a mixture of two separated uniform distributions at critical capacity, grows as \((\log T)^2\), confirming this as a tight bound.

Conclusion: Regret upper bounds of \((\log T)^2\) for gapped bounded-density distributions are tight, explaining why support gaps lead to larger regret.

Abstract: This paper studies additive regret in the multi-secretary problem, defined as the gap between the expected offline prophet reward and the reward of the best online policy. Prior work established \(O(\log T)\) regret for bounded-density distributions with connected support and \(O((\log T)^2)\) upper bounds for bounded-density distributions with support gaps. It was unknown whether the extra logarithmic factor is necessary even in the one-resource model. We prove that it is necessary. For a mixture of two separated uniform distributions at the critical capacity, the optimal regret grows at least on the order of \((\log T)^2\). Thus the existing \(O((\log T)^2)\) upper bounds for bounded-density gapped instances, including those implied by network revenue management models with continuous rewards, are tight in this simplest specialization. The same framework also yields a matching lower bound for gapped distributions whose gap-facing densities vanish near the support edges; this companion result is given in the appendix. The proofs use Bellman certificates: feasible solutions to a relaxation of the exact Bellman recursion. This framework converts lower bounds into explicit certificate constructions and identifies why support gaps permit larger regret.

</details>


<div id='cs.LO'></div>

# cs.LO [[Back]](#toc)

### [519] [ADVENT: LLM-Driven Automatic Predicate Invention for ILP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01585)
*Tingting Yu,Pei-Cing Huang,Chan Hsu,Chan-Tung Ku,Yihuang Kang*

Main category: cs.LO

TL;DR: ADVENT uses Large Language Models (LLMs) to streamline predicate invention (PI) in Inductive Logic Programming (ILP), addressing efficiency and cross-task reuse challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to overcome limitations in predicate invention for ILP, such as reliance on domain expertise and the generation of opaque predicates, which hinder adaptation and knowledge reuse.

Method: ADVENT combines LLM-driven abductive reasoning with Prolog deductive verification in an iterative loop to create and refine predicates. It also leverages LLMs to analyze relational data and accumulates invented predicates in a reusable knowledge pool.

Result: Experiments across seven LLMs and nine poker-hand concepts show a 58% success rate where traditional ILP fails, improved to 80% via verification, with up to +31 percentage points gained from the knowledge pool.

Conclusion: ADVENT demonstrates a significant leap in automating predicate invention, enabling cross-task knowledge reuse and producing interpretable rules, showcasing its potential in advancing ILP.

Abstract: Predicate invention (PI), the creation of new predicates to extend the hypothesis space, remains a critical bottleneck in Inductive Logic Programming (ILP). Existing methods rely on domain expertise and produce semantically opaque predicates, hindering adaptation to unfamiliar domains and cross-task reuse. We present ADVENT, an LLM-driven PI mechanism for ILP. ADVENT pairs LLM abductive generation with Prolog deductive verification, forming an iterative loop in which concrete execution results guide the LLM to refine candidate predicates. The mechanism leverages Large Language Models to identify implicit patterns in structured relational data and invent auxiliary predicates with meaningful names and definitions. Invented predicates and learned rules accumulate in a knowledge pool for cross-task reuse. Experiments on nine poker-hand concepts across seven LLMs show that LLM-driven PI achieves 58% success rate where ILP alone fails entirely, formal verification raises this to 80%, and the knowledge pool yields gains up to +31 percentage points, while producing human-interpretable rules. These results suggest that ADVENT offers a promising direction for automating predicate invention and enabling cross-task knowledge reuse in ILP.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [520] [Low-Latency Task-Oriented Image Transmission with Opportunistic Spectrum Access](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01921)
*João Henrique Inacio de Souza,Mattia Merluzzi,Mateus P. Mota,Beatriz Soret,Petar Popovski*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper presents a low-latency communication framework using opportunistic spectrum access and AI-driven receivers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional communication systems suffer from high latency and inefficiencies under limited spectrum and fading conditions, particularly for task-oriented applications.

Method: The framework uses a vector-quantized variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE) for compressing data and transmits it via idle licensed spectrum channels using standard digital modulation. A latency model incorporating compression and communication metrics is developed.

Result: The proposed approach reduces latency by up to 79- and 3.3-fold while maintaining classification accuracy drops of only 5.7% and 2.4%, outperforming conventional methods.

Conclusion: The proposed transmission framework enables task-oriented communication with significant reductions in latency and reliable performance under constrained channel conditions.

Abstract: Communication systems designed for reliable data reconstruction, rather than task-oriented communication, typically rely on separate source and channel coding and incur high latency under limited spectrum availability and fading channels. To address this, we propose a transmission framework with opportunistic spectrum access, in which the transmitter sends discrete latent representations learned via a vector-quantized variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE) over idle licensed channels using standard digital modulation. The AI-powered receiver is still able to reconstruct task-related information from the heavily compressed data. We develop a cross-layer latency model that accounts for compression, block errors, retransmissions, and stochastic channel access. Results on latency-accuracy trade-offs show that the proposed scheme achieves at least 79- and 3.3-fold latency reductions with only 5.7% and 2.4% drops in classification accuracy compared to benchmarks using conventional source and channel coding. The framework enables low-latency communication and reliable task execution even under limited spectrum availability and challenging channel conditions.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [521] [Aggregation with Exponential Weights is Optimal in Expectation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02247)
*Mikael Møller Høgsgaard,Patrick Rebeschini,Tobias Wegel*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: This paper confirms that the Aggregation with Exponential Weights (AEW) achieves minimax-rate optimal excess risk in expectation under random design, settling a longstanding conjecture by Lecué and Mendelson.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve whether the AEW estimator achieves minimax-rate optimal excess risk in expectation for large fixed temperatures under random design without requiring additional assumptions.

Method: The authors analyze the AEW estimator's performance with squared loss and prove its sharp phase transition properties using technical bounds such as temperature constraints that guarantee optimality.

Result: The AEW achieves excess risk of $T \log(M) / (n+1)$ in expectation under certain conditions, without needing Bernstein-type assumptions, validating a conjecture about AEW's behavior for sufficiently large temperatures.

Conclusion: AEW exhibits a sharp phase transition in performance based on temperature levels, confirming theoretical predictions about its optimality for large fixed values.

Abstract: The aggregation with exponential weights (AEW) estimator is not fully understood in the basic setting of model selection aggregation with squared loss. In particular, whether it is minimax-rate optimal in expectation for large enough fixed temperatures and under random design has been an open problem since its introduction, which was explicitly posed by Lecué and Mendelson (2013). In this paper, we settle this problem by showing that \emph{without} requiring a Bernstein-type assumption, the AEW indeed achieves the excess risk $T \log (M) / (n+1)$ in expectation, whenever the temperature $T$ satisfies $(L^2/T)\exp(B/T)\leq μ/2$. Here, the number of dictionary elements is $M$, the estimator has observed $n$ i.i.d. samples from any distribution, and the loss is assumed to be bounded by $B$, $L$-Lipschitz continuous and $μ$-strongly convex. For squared loss, we show that $T\geq 4 b^2$ suffices when the predictions and labels are $[0,b]$-valued. Because AEW is known to be suboptimal in expectation for temperatures below some constant, this shows that AEW has a sharp phase transition when the temperature is large enough but constant, as conjectured by Lecué and Mendelson.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [522] [Quantifying the Uncertainty of Blindly Estimated Room Embeddings Using a Dispersion-Calibrated Score](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01527)
*Yang Xiang,Philipp Götz,Emanuël A. P. Habets,Andreas Walther,Wenwu Wang,Philip J. B. Jackson*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method to create robust room embeddings from reverberant speech, improving their reliability despite variations in speech content or recording quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Room embeddings derived from reverberant speech are often unreliable due to the influence of speech content and recording degradation, which impairs performance in downstream tasks.

Method: The approach anchors embeddings to a structured room impulse response latent space using a multi-view data structure and KL-based alignment, refines robustness through multi-positive contrastive terms, and incorporates uncertainty quantification via a lightweight head optimized with rank-based objectives.

Result: The proposed method generates embeddings consistent across waveform- and spectrogram-level corruptions, providing selective prediction capabilities even when using a single utterance at inference.

Conclusion: This framework yields robust room embeddings and reliable uncertainty scoring, enhancing the applicability of reverberant speech analysis in practical scenarios.

Abstract: Room embeddings derived from reverberant speech are often unreliable: speech content and recording degradation can alter the representation even when speaker, room, and source-receiver geometry remain unchanged, degrading downstream task performance. We propose a framework that learns room embeddings robust to speech-content variation and a representation-level uncertainty score from reverberant speech without downstream-task supervision. The embedding is anchored to a structured room impulse response (RIR) latent space and trained using a multi-view data structure with Kullback-Leibler (KL)-based alignment; a multi-positive contrastive term further refines robustness. A lightweight uncertainty head is calibrated using the dispersion of corruption-induced embeddings and optimized with a rank-based objective. Across waveform- and spectrogram-level corruptions, the score is consistent with representation dispersion and enables effective selective prediction while requiring only a single utterance at inference.

</details>


### [523] [A Multi-Branch Hierarchy-Aware Framework for Heterogeneous Audio Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01974)
*Beile Ning,Jiayi Yu,Zitong Wang,Yufei Hu,Wenjun Xu,Yuanhang Qian,Zhongxin Bai,Gongping Huang*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper describes a system for Task 1 of the DCASE 2026 Challenge, focusing on classifying audio recordings using hierarchical approaches and achieving strong performance (Hierarchical F1 score of over 81%) through model innovation and ensemble techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of accurately classifying heterogeneous audio recordings while maintaining consistency with hierarchical taxonomies, a key requirement in the DCASE 2026 Challenge.

Method: The authors employed CLAP-based audio-text representations and improved the system in three ways: expanding the dataset with BSD35k, adding feature-specific acoustic branches, and refining predictions via hierarchy-aware classifiers and KNN post-processing. Ensemble models were also constructed to boost performance.

Result: The system achieved high hierarchical F1 scores: 80.84% for the best single model and over 81% for ensemble systems, showing noticeable improvements over the baseline.

Conclusion: The report demonstrates an effective approach to audio classification using hierarchical methods and ensemble strategies, illustrating the benefits of dataset expansion, feature-specific modeling, and advanced post-processing techniques.

Abstract: This technical report describes our system for Task 1 of the DCASE 2026 Challenge, which aims to classify heterogeneous audio recordings according to the Broad Sound Taxonomy (BST). The task requires both accurate second-level prediction and consistency with the top-level taxonomy.
